definitions.label: ai system
definitions.description: means a machine-based system that is designed to operate with varying levels of autonomy and that may exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and that, for explicit or implicit objectives, infers, from the input it receives, how to generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions that can influence physical or virtual environments;

definitions.label: risk
definitions.description: means the combination of the probability of an occurrence of harm and the severity of that harm;

definitions.label: provider
definitions.description: means a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body that develops an AI system or a general-purpose AI model or that has an AI system or a general-purpose AI model developed and places it on the market or puts the AI system into service under its own name or trademark, whether for payment or free of charge;

definitions.label: deployer
definitions.description: means a natural or legal person, public authority, agency or other body using an AI system under its authority except where the AI system is used in the course of a personal non-professional activity;

definitions.label: authorised representative
definitions.description: means a natural or legal person located or established in the Union who has received and accepted a written mandate from a provider of an AI system or a general-purpose AI model to, respectively, perform and carry out on its behalf the obligations and procedures established by this Regulation;

definitions.label: importer
definitions.description: means a natural or legal person located or established in the Union that places on the market an AI system that bears the name or trademark of a natural or legal person established in a third country;

definitions.label: distributor
definitions.description: means a natural or legal person in the supply chain, other than the provider or the importer, that makes an AI system available on the Union market;

definitions.label: operator
definitions.description: means a provider, product manufacturer, deployer, authorised representative, importer or distributor;

definitions.label: placing on the market
definitions.description: means the first making available of an AI system or a general-purpose AI model on the Union market;

definitions.label: making available on the market
definitions.description: means the supply of an AI system or a general-purpose AI model for distribution or use on the Union market in the course of a commercial activity, whether in return for payment or free of charge;

definitions.label: putting into service
definitions.description: means the supply of an AI system for first use directly to the deployer or for own use in the Union for its intended purpose;

definitions.label: intended purpose
definitions.description: means the use for which an AI system is intended by the provider, including the specific context and conditions of use, as specified in the information supplied by the provider in the instructions for use, promotional or sales materials and statements, as well as in the technical documentation;

definitions.label: reasonably foreseeable misuse
definitions.description: means the use of an AI system in a way that is not in accordance with its intended purpose, but which may result from reasonably foreseeable human behaviour or interaction with other systems, including other AI systems;

definitions.label: safety component
definitions.description: means a component of a product or of an AI system which fulfils a safety function for that product or AI system, or the failure or malfunctioning of which endangers the health and safety of persons or property;

definitions.label: instructions for use
definitions.description: means the information provided by the provider to inform the deployer of, in particular, an AI system's intended purpose and proper use;

definitions.label: recall of an ai system
definitions.description: means any measure aiming to achieve the return to the provider or taking out of service or disabling the use of an AI system made available to deployers;

definitions.label: withdrawal of an ai system
definitions.description: means any measure aiming to prevent an AI system in the supply chain being made available on the market;

definitions.label: performance of an ai system
definitions.description: means the ability of an AI system to achieve its intended purpose;

definitions.label: notifying authority
definitions.description: means the national authority responsible for setting up and carrying out the necessary procedures for the assessment, designation and notification of conformity assessment bodies and for their monitoring;

definitions.label: conformity assessment
definitions.description: means the process of demonstrating whether the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2 relating to a high-risk AI system have been fulfilled;

definitions.label: conformity assessment body
definitions.description: means a body that performs third-party conformity assessment activities, including testing, certification and inspection;

definitions.label: notified body
definitions.description: means a conformity assessment body notified in accordance with this Regulation and other relevant Union harmonisation legislation;

definitions.label: substantial modification
definitions.description: means a change to an AI system after its placing on the market or putting into service which is not foreseen or planned in the initial conformity assessment carried out by the provider and as a result of which the compliance of the AI system with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2 is affected or results in a modification to the intended purpose for which the AI system has been assessed;

definitions.label: ce marking
definitions.description: means a marking by which a provider indicates that an AI system is in conformity with the requirements set out in Chapter III, Section 2 and other applicable Union harmonisation legislation providing for its affixing;

definitions.label: post-market monitoring system
definitions.description: means all activities carried out by providers of AI systems to collect and review experience gained from the use of AI systems they place on the market or put into service for the purpose of identifying any need to immediately apply any necessary corrective or preventive actions;

definitions.label: market surveillance authority
definitions.description: means the national authority carrying out the activities and taking the measures pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2019/1020;

definitions.label: harmonised standard
definitions.description: means a harmonised standard as defined in Article 2(1), point \(c\), of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012;

definitions.label: common specification
definitions.description: means a set of technical specifications as defined in Article 2, point (4) of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, providing means to comply with certain requirements established under this Regulation;

definitions.label: training data
definitions.description: means data used for training an AI system through fitting its learnable parameters;

definitions.label: validation data
definitions.description: means data used for providing an evaluation of the trained AI system and for tuning its non-learnable parameters and its learning process in order, inter alia, to prevent underfitting or overfitting;

definitions.label: validation data set
definitions.description: means a separate data set or part of the training data set, either as a fixed or variable split;

definitions.label: testing data
definitions.description: means data used for providing an independent evaluation of the AI system in order to confirm the expected performance of that system before its placing on the market or putting into service;

definitions.label: input data
definitions.description: means data provided to or directly acquired by an AI system on the basis of which the system produces an output;

definitions.label: biometric data
definitions.description: means personal data resulting from specific technical processing relating to the physical, physiological or behavioural characteristics of a natural person, such as facial images or dactyloscopic data;

definitions.label: biometric identification
definitions.description: means the automated recognition of physical, physiological, behavioural, or psychological human features for the purpose of establishing the identity of a natural person by comparing biometric data of that individual to biometric data of individuals stored in a database;

definitions.label: biometric verification
definitions.description: means the automated, one-to-one verification, including authentication, of the identity of natural persons by comparing their biometric data to previously provided biometric data;

definitions.label: special categories of personal data
definitions.description: means the categories of personal data referred to in Article 9(1) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679, Article 10 of Directive (EU) 2016/680 and Article 10(1) of Regulation (EU) 2018/1725;

definitions.label: sensitive operational data
definitions.description: means operational data related to activities of prevention, detection, investigation or prosecution of criminal offences, the disclosure of which could jeopardise the integrity of criminal proceedings;

definitions.label: emotion recognition system
definitions.description: means an AI system for the purpose of identifying or inferring emotions or intentions of natural persons on the basis of their biometric data;

definitions.label: biometric categorisation system
definitions.description: means an AI system for the purpose of assigning natural persons to specific categories on the basis of their biometric data, unless it is ancillary to another commercial service and strictly necessary for objective technical reasons;

definitions.label: remote biometric identification system
definitions.description: means an AI system for the purpose of identifying natural persons, without their active involvement, typically at a distance through the comparison of a person's biometric data with the biometric data contained in a reference database;

definitions.label: real-time remote biometric identification system
definitions.description: means a remote biometric identification system, whereby the capturing of biometric data, the comparison and the identification all occur without a significant delay, comprising not only instant identification, but also limited short delays in order to avoid circumvention;

definitions.label: post-remote biometric identification system
definitions.description: means a remote biometric identification system other than a real-time remote biometric identification system;

definitions.label: publicly accessible space
definitions.description: means any publicly or privately owned physical place accessible to an undetermined number of natural persons, regardless of whether certain conditions for access may apply, and regardless of the potential capacity restrictions;

definitions.label: law enforcement authority
law enforcement
definitions.description: means activities carried out by law enforcement authorities or on their behalf for the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including safeguarding against and preventing threats to public security;

definitions.label: ai office
definitions.description: means the Commission's function of contributing to the implementation, monitoring and supervision of AI systems and general-purpose AI models, and AI governance, provided for in Commission Decision of 24 January 2024; references in this Regulation to the AI Office shall be construed as references to the Commission;

definitions.label: national competent authority
definitions.description: means a notifying authority or a market surveillance authority; as regards AI systems put into service or used by Union institutions, agencies, offices and bodies, references to national competent authorities or market surveillance authorities in this Regulation shall be construed as references to the European Data Protection Supervisor;

definitions.label: serious incident
personal data
definitions.description: means personal data as defined in Article 4, point (1), of Regulation (EU) 2016/679;

definitions.label: non-personal data
definitions.description: means data other than personal data as defined in Article 4, point (1), of Regulation (EU) 2016/679;

definitions.label: profiling
definitions.description: means profiling as defined in Article 4, point (4), of Regulation (EU) 2016/679;

definitions.label: real-world testing plan
definitions.description: means a document that describes the objectives, methodology, geographical, population and temporal scope, monitoring, organisation and conduct of testing in real-world conditions;

definitions.label: sandbox plan
definitions.description: means a document agreed between the participating provider and the competent authority describing the objectives, conditions, timeframe, methodology and requirements for the activities carried out within the sandbox;

definitions.label: ai regulatory sandbox
definitions.description: means a controlled framework set up by a competent authority which offers providers or prospective providers of AI systems the possibility to develop, train, validate and test, where appropriate in real-world conditions, an innovative AI system, pursuant to a sandbox plan for a limited time under regulatory supervision;

definitions.label: ai literacy
definitions.description: means skills, knowledge and understanding that allow providers, deployers and affected persons, taking into account their respective rights and obligations in the context of this Regulation, to make an informed deployment of AI systems, as well as to gain awareness about the opportunities and risks of AI and possible harm it can cause;

definitions.label: testing in real-world conditions
definitions.description: means the temporary testing of an AI system for its intended purpose in real-world conditions outside a laboratory or otherwise simulated environment, with a view to gathering reliable and robust data and to assessing and verifying the conformity of the AI system with the requirements of this Regulation and it does not qualify as placing the AI system on the market or putting it into service within the meaning of this Regulation, provided that all the conditions laid down in Article 57 or 60 are fulfilled;

definitions.label: subject
definitions.description: , for the purpose of real-world testing, means a natural person who participates in testing in real-world conditions;

definitions.label: informed consent
definitions.description: means a subject's freely given, specific, unambiguous and voluntary expression of his or her willingness to participate in a particular testing in real-world conditions, after having been informed of all aspects of the testing that are relevant to the subject's decision to participate;

definitions.label: deep fake
definitions.description: means AI-generated or manipulated image, audio or video content that resembles existing persons, objects, places, entities or events and would falsely appear to a person to be authentic or truthful;

definitions.label: widespread infringement
critical infrastructure
definitions.description: means critical infrastructure as defined in Article 2, point (4), of Directive (EU) 2022/2557;

definitions.label: general-purpose ai model
definitions.description: means an AI model, including where such an AI model is trained with a large amount of data using self-supervision at scale, that displays significant generality and is capable of competently performing a wide range of distinct tasks regardless of the way the model is placed on the market and that can be integrated into a variety of downstream systems or applications, except AI models that are used for research, development or prototyping activities before they are placed on the market;

definitions.label: high-impact capabilities
definitions.description: means capabilities that match or exceed the capabilities recorded in the most advanced general-purpose AI models;

definitions.label: systemic risk
definitions.description: means a risk that is specific to the high-impact capabilities of general-purpose AI models, having a significant impact on the Union market due to their reach, or due to actual or reasonably foreseeable negative effects on public health, safety, public security, fundamental rights, or the society as a whole, that can be propagated at scale across the value chain;

definitions.label: general-purpose ai system
definitions.description: means an AI system which is based on a general-purpose AI model and which has the capability to serve a variety of purposes, both for direct use as well as for integration in other AI systems;

definitions.label: floating-point operation
definitions.description: means any mathematical operation or assignment involving floating-point numbers, which are a subset of the real numbers typically represented on computers by an integer of fixed precision scaled by an integer exponent of a fixed base;

definitions.label: downstream provider
definitions.description: means a provider of an AI system, including a general-purpose AI system, which integrates an AI model, regardless of whether the AI model is provided by themselves and vertically integrated or provided by another entity based on contractual relations.

chapters.heading: Chapter I
chapters.title: GENERAL PROVISIONS
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter I: GENERAL PROVISIONS

chapters.heading: Article 1
chapters.title: Subject matter
chapters.fullTitle: Article 1: Subject matter
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the regulation's purpose: improving the internal market and promoting trustworthy AI while protecting health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy, rule of law, and the environment. Enumerates the seven categories of rules this regulation lays down.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Declaratory article stating the regulation's purpose and enumerating its contents. No operative obligations, no conditional language, no discretionary terms.
Purpose statement. Declaratory, non-operative. No obligations or conditions.
Enumerative clause listing what the regulation lays down. No operative obligations, no conditional or discretionary language.
Enumerative sub-point under Article 1(2). Declaratory, listing a category of rules the regulation contains.
Enumerative sub-point. Declaratory reference to prohibitions covered elsewhere in the regulation.
Enumerative sub-point. Declaratory reference to high-risk requirements and operator obligations covered elsewhere.
Enumerative sub-point. Declaratory reference to transparency rules covered elsewhere.
Enumerative sub-point. Declaratory reference to GPAI model rules.
Enumerative sub-point. Declaratory reference to governance and enforcement rules.
Enumerative sub-point. Declaratory reference to innovation support measures.

chapters.heading: Article 2
chapters.title: Scope
chapters.fullTitle: Article 2: Scope
chapters.summaryText: Defines who and what falls within the regulation's scope, including territorial reach to third-country providers whose AI output is used in the Union. Establishes twelve paragraphs of inclusions, exclusions, and carve-outs covering military/national security, research, open-source, personal use, and existing Union law.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-2 is a definitional scope article and does not impose direct obligations subject to penalties; violations would arise from conduct under substantive provisions

ambiguitySignal.reason: Scope boundaries are generally clear but several exclusions use language requiring interpretation: 'exclusively for military, defence or national security purposes' (paragraph 3), 'adequate safeguards' (paragraph 4), 'sole purpose of scientific research' (paragraph 6). These terms are not further defined in the regulation.
Enumerative list of entities the regulation applies to. Each category is defined in Article 3. No discretionary language.
Defines a specific actor category in scope. Terms are formally defined in Article 3.
Defines a specific actor category in scope. Terms are formally defined in Article 3.

The phrase 'output produced by the AI system is used in the Union' requires interpretation of what constitutes 'use' and when the output is deemed to be 'in the Union' (e.g., cross-border data flows, remote services).
Defines specific actor categories in scope. Terms are formally defined in Article 3.
Defines a specific actor category in scope (product manufacturers as providers). Terms are formally defined in Article 3.
Defines a specific actor category in scope. Terms are formally defined in Article 3.
Defines a specific category of persons in scope (affected persons located in the Union). Clear territorial criterion.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Specifies limited application of this Regulation to AI systems already regulated under specific Union harmonisation legislation. Scope is precisely bounded by reference to Annex I Section B and enumerated articles.

where.text: areas outside the scope of Union law
placed on the market, put into service, or used exclusively for military, defence or national security purposes

Explicit minimum harmonization clause preserving Member States' ability to adopt more protective worker rights provisions. Clear intent and scope.

chapters.heading: Article 3
chapters.title: Definitions
chapters.fullTitle: Article 3: Definitions
chapters.summaryText: Provides 68 definitions of terms used throughout the regulation, including AI system, provider, deployer, operator, risk categories, biometric systems, general-purpose AI models, and key processes like placing on the market and conformity assessment.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Definitional article providing foundational terminology. Each definition is explicitly stated. No operative obligations, conditions, or discretionary language. Definitions themselves are referenced throughout the regulation.

chapters.heading: Article 4
chapters.title: AI literacy
chapters.fullTitle: Article 4: AI literacy
chapters.summaryText: Requires providers and deployers to ensure sufficient AI literacy of staff and persons operating AI systems on their behalf, proportionate to technical knowledge, experience, context, and affected persons.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Article 99 does not list Article 4 among penalized obligations. No direct penalty exposure identified.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The obligation uses intentionally broad qualifiers: 'to their best extent', 'sufficient level', 'taking into account'. These provide significant discretion to operators on what constitutes compliance, making the standard flexible but vague.

chapters.heading: Chapter II
chapters.title: PROHIBITED AI PRACTICES
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter II: PROHIBITED AI PRACTICES

chapters.heading: Article 5
chapters.title: Prohibited AI practices
chapters.fullTitle: Article 5: Prohibited AI practices
chapters.summaryText: Prohibits eight categories of AI practices deemed unacceptable due to fundamental rights violations. Includes manipulation, exploitation of vulnerabilities, social scoring, predictive policing, untargeted facial scraping, emotion recognition in workplace/education, biometric categorization, and real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces (with narrow law enforcement exceptions requiring prior authorization, impact assessment, and reporting).

ambiguitySignal.reason: While the eight prohibited categories are enumerated, key terms like 'materially distorting behaviour', 'significant harm', 'purposefully manipulative', 'vulnerabilities', and 'strictly necessary' require case-by-case interpretation. Real-time biometric exception conditions (paragraphs 1(h), 2, 3) involve balancing tests and proportionality assessments.

Framework paragraph listing eight prohibited categories. Each sub-point contains qualitative thresholds ('materially distorting', 'significant harm', 'purposefully manipulative') requiring interpretation in specific contexts.

Terms 'subliminal techniques beyond consciousness', 'purposefully manipulative', 'materially distorting', 'appreciably impairing', and 'significant harm' lack precise thresholds. Causation test ('causing them to take a decision they would not have otherwise taken') inherently counterfactual.

'Exploits vulnerabilities' and 'specific social or economic situation' are context-dependent. What constitutes exploitation versus lawful personalization is not clearly delineated. 'Materially distorting behaviour' and 'significant harm' require case-by-case assessment.

'Social behaviour', 'inferred or predicted personal characteristics', 'social score', and 'contexts unrelated to where data was generated' require interpretation. Thresholds for 'detrimental', 'unjustified', and 'disproportionate' treatment are not quantified. Boundary between lawful creditworthiness assessment and prohibited social scoring is unclear.

Prohibition applies when risk assessment is 'based solely on profiling' or 'assessing personality traits'. Exception for systems 'supporting human assessment' based on 'objective and verifiable facts directly linked to criminal activity' introduces boundary questions: what degree of AI involvement crosses from support to sole basis? What counts as 'objective and verifiable'?

'Untargeted scraping' versus targeted collection is not precisely defined. Whether bulk download from a single website or aggregation across multiple sources constitutes 'untargeted' may require interpretation. 'Create or expand' suggests even incremental additions to existing databases are prohibited, but boundary with lawful facial recognition system development is unclear.

Prohibition applies to 'infer emotions' in 'workplace and education institutions', except for 'medical or safety reasons'. What qualifies as inferring emotion versus detecting affect or engagement is not defined. The scope of 'medical or safety reasons' is open to interpretation (e.g., driver fatigue monitoring, student wellbeing tools).

Prohibition applies to 'deduce or infer' sensitive attributes from biometric data. Exception for 'labelling or filtering of lawfully acquired biometric datasets' and 'categorizing in law enforcement' lacks clear boundaries. What constitutes deduction/inference versus technical data labelling is not specified. Scope of law enforcement exception (which sensitive categories, which contexts) is unclear.

referencesWithContext.context: the localisation or identification of a person suspected of having committed a criminal offence, for the purpose of conducting a criminal investigation or prosecution or executing a criminal penalty for offences referred to in [Annex II](#annex-ii)
referencesWithContext.snippet: for offences referred to in Annex II and punishable in the Member State concerned

ambiguitySignal.reason: Prohibition has three narrow exceptions requiring 'strictly necessary' for: (i) victim search, (ii) preventing 'specific, substantial and imminent threat' or 'genuine and present/foreseeable terrorist attack', (iii) locating suspects of Annex II offences. Terms 'strictly necessary', 'specific/substantial/imminent', 'genuine and present/foreseeable' are balancing tests without precise criteria. Exception (iii) limited by minimum 4-year custodial sentence threshold, but this varies by Member State.

These are intentionally broad criteria for proportionality assessment. 'Necessary and proportionate safeguards' and 'temporal, geographic and personal limitations' are not quantified. 'Duly justified cases of urgency' allowing delayed registration lacks specific criteria.
Requires assessing 'seriousness, probability and scale of harm' if system not used. No thresholds, weighting, or scoring criteria provided. Inherently prospective and counterfactual.

chapters.heading: Chapter III

chapters.heading: Article 6
chapters.title: Classification rules for high-risk AI systems
chapters.fullTitle: Article 6: Classification rules for high-risk AI systems
chapters.summaryText: Article 6 establishes the classification framework for high-risk AI systems, including those used as safety components under EU harmonisation legislation (Annex I) requiring third-party conformity assessment, and those listed in Annex III. It provides conditions under which Annex III systems may not be considered high-risk and grants the Commission delegated powers to amend these conditions.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 6(3) derogation conditions may be amended by Commission delegated acts (paragraphs 6-7), and Commission guidelines (paragraph 5) will specify practical implementation. The concept of 'significant risk of harm' and 'materially influencing the outcome' requires interpretive application.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 6(1) and corresponding obligations apply from 2027-08-02. All other paragraphs apply from 2026-08-02 per default Article 113 timeline.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Determining whether an AI system qualifies as a 'safety component' or whether it 'materially influences' a product's safety requires case-by-case technical assessment. The scope of Annex I legislation and conformity assessment requirements is complex.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 6(1) and corresponding obligations apply from 2027-08-02 per Article 113(3).

referencesWithContext.context: the AI system is intended to be used as a safety component of a product, or the AI system is itself a product, covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I
referencesWithContext.snippet: ion harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I;

ambiguitySignal.reason: The concept of 'safety component' is not defined in the AI Act but depends on the interpretation within the context of various Annex I harmonisation acts (e.g., Machinery Regulation, Medical Devices Regulation). What constitutes 'intended use' as a safety component requires domain-specific technical judgment.

referencesWithContext.context: the product whose safety component pursuant to point (a) is the AI system, or the AI system itself as a product, is required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment, with a view to the placing on the market or the putting into service of that product pursuant to the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I
referencesWithContext.snippet: ion harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Whether a product is 'required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment' depends on risk classification within each specific Annex I legislative act. The trigger for high-risk classification thus requires cross-referencing multiple regulatory frameworks.

referencesWithContext.snippet: ticle-6-1), AI systems referred to in Annex III shall be considered to be high-risk.

ambiguitySignal.reason: High-risk classification depends on the enumerated use cases in Annex III, which may be amended by the Commission under Article 7. Whether a specific AI system falls within an Annex III use case requires case-by-case assessment.

referencesWithContext.context: By derogation from paragraph 2, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk where it does not pose a significant risk of harm

referencesWithContext.snippet: cle-6-2), an AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-ris

ambiguitySignal.reason: Key terms are highly subjective and context-dependent: 'significant risk of harm', 'materially influencing the outcome', 'narrow procedural task', 'improve the result', 'detect patterns', 'proper human review'. These require case-by-case judgment without precise thresholds. Article 6(5) Commission guidelines will provide practical examples, but fundamental ambiguity remains.

The term 'narrow procedural task' is not defined. What qualifies as 'narrow' versus substantive, and what constitutes a 'procedural' versus decisional task, requires interpretive judgment. Commission guidelines (Article 6(5)) will provide examples but not a precise definition.

The phrase 'improve the result of a previously completed human activity' is undefined. It is unclear what qualifies as 'improvement' (efficiency, accuracy, formatting?) and what temporal or procedural relationship to the 'previously completed' activity is required.

The condition requires that the system 'detect decision-making patterns or deviations' but 'is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment, without proper human review'. The phrase 'proper human review' is undefined, and distinguishing between 'detecting patterns' and 'influencing assessment' is subtle and context-dependent.

referencesWithContext.context: the AI system is intended to perform a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purposes of the use cases listed in Annex III
referencesWithContext.snippet: e purposes of the use cases listed in Annex III.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The term 'preparatory task' is undefined. The boundary between preparatory and substantive tasks is unclear. Additionally, the exception-to-the-exception (profiling always high-risk) uses 'profiling of natural persons', which is defined in Article 3(41) but requires interpretation in specific contexts.

What constitutes sufficient documentation to satisfy 'upon request of national competent authorities' is subject to authority judgment.

chapters.title: Amendments to Annex III
chapters.fullTitle: Article 7: Amendments to Annex III
chapters.summaryText: Empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts to add or remove high-risk AI system use-cases from Annex III based on specified risk criteria and conditions.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The substantive criteria for amending Annex III are detailed in paragraphs 1 and 2, but the actual exercise of this power is delegated to the Commission through delegated acts per Article 97, which will provide further specificity.

ambiguitySignal.reason: While the two conditions are clearly stated, determining whether an AI system poses risks 'equivalent to, or greater than' existing high-risk systems (point b) requires interpretive judgment informed by the criteria in paragraph 2.

referencesWithContext.snippet: isk AI systems already referred to in Annex III.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Determining whether risks are 'equivalent to, or greater than' those posed by existing high-risk systems requires comparative risk assessment using the criteria in Article 7(2), which involves judgment.

referencesWithContext.context: 2. When assessing the condition under [paragraph 1](#article-7-1), point (b), the Commission shall take into account the following criteria:
referencesWithContext.snippet: When assessing the condition under paragraph 1, point (b), the Commission shall tak

when.text: assessing the condition under [paragraph 1](#article-7-1), point (b)

ambiguitySignal.reason: The Commission 'shall take into account' the listed criteria, but the weight and balancing of these eleven multi-factor criteria requires interpretive judgment. The criteria themselves contain evaluative terms like 'extent', 'likelihood', and 'potential'.
The intended purpose of an AI system is a factual determination based on the provider's documentation and marketing.
Assessing 'the extent to which an AI system has been used or is likely to be used' requires both historical data analysis and predictive judgment about future adoption.
The nature and amount of data processed is an assessable technical characteristic. Special categories of personal data are defined in GDPR Article 9.

Measuring 'the extent to which the AI system acts autonomously' and the 'possibility for a human to override' requires technical assessment and judgment about what constitutes meaningful override capability.

Evaluating the extent of actual harm or significant concerns requires assessment of reports and allegations, which involves weighing evidence quality and determining what constitutes 'significant concerns'.
Assessing the 'potential extent' of harm, its 'intensity', and whether impact is 'disproportionate' on particular groups requires evaluative judgment and normative assessment.
Determining whether it is 'reasonably possible to opt-out' requires judgment about what constitutes practical and legal feasibility in specific contexts.
Assessing the 'extent' of power imbalance or vulnerability requires normative judgment informed by multiple factors (status, authority, knowledge, economic circumstances, age).

Determining whether outcomes are 'easily corrigible or reversible' requires technical assessment, and the provision explicitly notes that outcomes affecting health, safety, or fundamental rights 'shall not be considered' easily corrigible, which adds a normative constraint.

Assessing 'magnitude and likelihood of benefit' requires predictive judgment and normative balancing against potential risks. Benefits for 'individuals, groups, or society at large' involve different scales of analysis.

Evaluating the 'extent' to which existing law provides 'effective measures' requires legal analysis of sectoral regulations and judgment about what constitutes effectiveness in preventing or minimizing specific risks.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Determining whether a high-risk AI system 'no longer poses any significant risks' and whether deletion would 'not decrease the overall level of protection' requires comparative risk assessment and normative judgment.

referencesWithContext.context: s to fundamental rights, health or safety, taking into account the criteria listed in [paragraph 2](#article-7-2);
referencesWithContext.snippet: into account the criteria listed in paragraph 2;

ambiguitySignal.reason: Determining that a system 'no longer poses any significant risks' requires applying the Article 7(2) criteria and making a threshold judgment about what constitutes 'significant' risk.
Assessing whether deletion would 'decrease the overall level of protection' under Union law requires systemic analysis of how the broader legal framework would be affected by the removal.

chapters.heading: Section 2
chapters.title: Requirements for high-risk AI systems
chapters.fullTitle: Section 2: Requirements for high-risk AI systems

chapters.heading: Article 8
chapters.title: Compliance with the requirements
chapters.fullTitle: Article 8: Compliance with the requirements
chapters.summaryText: Article 8 establishes the baseline obligation that high-risk AI systems must comply with all requirements in Chapter III Section 2, taking into account intended purpose and state of the art. For products subject to both this Regulation and other Union harmonisation legislation, providers may integrate compliance processes to avoid duplication.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The phrase 'generally acknowledged state of the art on AI and AI-related technologies' requires interpretation of what constitutes state of the art at any given time, and how it applies to specific system types. The integration of compliance processes for Union harmonisation legislation also requires judgment about what is 'appropriate'.

referencesWithContext.context: of the art on AI and AI-related technologies. The risk management system referred to in [Article 9](#article-9) shall be taken into account when ensuring compliance with those requirements.
referencesWithContext.snippet: risk management system referred to in Article 9 shall be taken into account when ensur

when.text: ensuring compliance with those requirements

ambiguitySignal.reason: The 'generally acknowledged state of the art on AI and AI-related technologies' is a time-variant and context-dependent standard requiring expert judgment and consideration of evolving technical capabilities.

referencesWithContext.context: ion as well as requirements of the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of [Annex I](#annex-i) apply, providers shall be responsible for ensuring that their product is fully compliant w
referencesWithContext.snippet: tion legislation listed in Section A of Annex I apply, providers shall be responsible fo

referencesWithContext.context: sation legislation. In ensuring the compliance of high-risk AI systems referred to in [paragraph 1](#article-8-1) with the requirements set out in this Section, and in order to ensure consistency, avo
referencesWithContext.snippet: high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1 with the requirements set out in thi

where.text: a product contains an AI system, to which the requirements of this Regulation as well as requirements of the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Section A of Annex I apply

ambiguitySignal.reason: Providers have discretion ('have a choice') in how to integrate compliance processes. The terms 'as appropriate', 'necessary', and the scope of what constitutes proper integration require judgment based on the specific product and applicable legislation.

chapters.heading: Article 9
chapters.title: Risk management system
chapters.fullTitle: Article 9: Risk management system
chapters.summaryText: Establishes mandatory risk management requirements for high-risk AI systems. Requires a continuous, lifecycle-long process that identifies, evaluates, and mitigates risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights through testing and appropriate measures.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-a → article-16 (obligations of providers) → article-9 (provider obligations within Section 2)

ambiguitySignal.reason: While the requirement for a risk management system is clear, key terms like 'appropriate' risk management measures, 'reasonably foreseeable risks', 'acceptable' residual risk, and 'adequate' design require judgment and may be interpreted differently in specific contexts.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113 (general application date for Chapter III)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Unambiguous obligation to establish, implement, document and maintain a risk management system. The 'what' is clear, though 'how' is specified in subsequent paragraphs.

when.text: the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose

ambiguitySignal.reason: Clearly defines the four required steps of the risk management process. The obligation is specific and the enumerated steps are well-defined.

The requirement is clear, but 'reasonably foreseeable risks' is a judgment-based standard that requires interpretation in specific contexts. What is 'reasonably foreseeable' may vary by application domain and state of knowledge.

when.text: the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose

ambiguitySignal.reason: Contains the phrase 'reasonably foreseeable misuse', which requires judgment about what misuse scenarios are 'reasonably foreseeable' for a given system. This is context-dependent and subject to interpretation.

referencesWithContext.context: on the analysis of data gathered from the post-market monitoring system referred to in [Article 72](#article-72);
referencesWithContext.snippet: ket monitoring system referred to in Article 72;

ambiguitySignal.reason: Clear requirement to evaluate risks based on post-market monitoring data. The obligation is well-defined and linked to Article 72.
Uses 'appropriate and targeted' risk management measures, both of which are inherently subjective and context-dependent terms. What is 'appropriate' requires judgment and may be debated.
Limits the scope of risks to those 'reasonably mitigated or eliminated' through development or design. The term 'reasonably' requires judgment about technical feasibility and proportionality.

referencesWithContext.context: 4. The risk management measures referred to in [paragraph 2](#article-9-2), point (d), shall give due consideration to the effects and possible interaction resulting from the
referencesWithContext.snippet: management measures referred to in paragraph 2, point (d), shall give due considera

referencesWithContext.snippet: management measures referred to in paragraph 2, point (d), shall be such that the r

ambiguitySignal.reason: Central concept of 'acceptable' residual risk is inherently subjective and not defined in the regulation. What constitutes 'acceptable' risk depends on context, stakeholder perspectives, and societal values.

referencesWithContext.snippet: dentified and evaluated pursuant to paragraph 2 in as far as technically feasible th

ambiguitySignal.reason: Requires elimination or reduction 'in as far as technically feasible'. What is 'technically feasible' is a context-dependent and evolving standard that requires expert judgment.
Uses 'where appropriate' and 'adequate' mitigation measures, both discretionary and subjective standards. No criteria provided for determining appropriateness or adequacy.

Clear obligation to test high-risk AI systems for risk management and compliance. The purpose of testing is well-defined.

Requires testing 'as appropriate' during development and against 'prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds that are appropriate' to the intended purpose. The term 'appropriate' appears twice without clear criteria.

Clear permission for providers to integrate AI Act risk management with existing risk management under other Union law. The optional nature ('may') is explicit.

chapters.heading: Article 10
chapters.title: Data and data governance
chapters.fullTitle: Article 10: Data and data governance
chapters.summaryText: Establishes data quality and governance requirements for high-risk AI systems that use training-based models. Mandates appropriate data governance practices covering design, collection, preparation, bias detection/mitigation, and gap identification. Permits exceptional processing of special categories of personal data for bias correction under strict safeguards.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-a → article-16 → providers of high-risk AI systems (Article 10 obligations are part of Chapter III Section 2 provider requirements)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Contains several evaluative standards: 'appropriate' data governance practices (paragraph 2), 'relevant', 'sufficiently representative', 'to the best extent possible' free of errors (paragraph 3), 'appropriate statistical properties' (paragraph 3). These require professional judgment to apply.
Straightforward conditional obligation: IF a high-risk AI system uses training-based techniques, THEN it must be developed using quality data sets meeting paragraphs 2-5 criteria.

Requires 'appropriate' data governance practices without defining 'appropriate'. The eight enumerated practices (points a-h) provide guidance but still require professional judgment about what constitutes appropriateness for a specific system's intended purpose.
'Relevant design choices' is intentionally broad, not further specified. Allows flexibility but provides minimal concrete guidance.
Specifies concrete governance concerns: data collection processes, origin, and for personal data the original collection purpose. These are factual documentation requirements.
Lists specific data preparation operations. Concrete and enumerated, though not exhaustive (uses 'such as').
Requires formulation and documentation of assumptions about what data measures and represents. Concrete procedural requirement.
Requires assessment of availability, quantity, and 'suitability' of data sets. While availability and quantity are objective, 'suitability' requires judgment against intended purpose.

Requires examination of 'possible biases that are likely to affect' health, safety, fundamental rights, or cause discrimination. 'Possible' and 'likely' introduce uncertainty. What threshold of likelihood triggers action is not specified.
'Appropriate measures' to detect, prevent, and mitigate biases is intentionally broad. No criteria for what makes a measure 'appropriate' or what threshold of bias requires mitigation.
Requires identification of data gaps/shortcomings that prevent compliance and how to address them. Concrete procedural requirement linked to regulatory compliance.

Sets quality criteria but uses evaluative language: 'relevant', 'sufficiently representative', 'to the best extent possible' free of errors, 'appropriate' statistical properties. Thresholds for 'sufficient', 'best extent', and 'appropriate' require judgment.

Requires data sets to account for characteristics 'particular to the specific geographical, contextual, behavioural or functional setting' but 'to the extent required by the intended purpose'. What constitutes 'particular' characteristics and 'extent required' involves interpretation.

where.text: strictly necessary for the purpose of ensuring bias detection and correction
all conditions in points (a) through (f) must be met

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-a → article-16 → article-10-5 (if conditions are violated, the permission becomes a violation of provider obligations)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Permission is conditional on 'strictly necessary' (high bar but requires interpretation) and 'appropriate safeguards' (point b: 'state-of-the-art security', point c: 'suitable safeguards'). What constitutes 'state-of-the-art' and 'suitable' evolves and requires expert judgment.

Requires demonstrating that bias detection/correction 'cannot be effectively fulfilled' by other data. 'Effectively' is a standard requiring judgment about technical feasibility and performance thresholds.

Requires 'technical limitations on the re-use' and 'state-of-the-art security and privacy-preserving measures'. 'State-of-the-art' is a moving target requiring continuous assessment of current best practices.

Requires data be 'secured, protected, subject to suitable safeguards' with 'strict controls'. What constitutes 'suitable' safeguards and 'strict' controls depends on context and evolving security standards.
Absolute prohibition on transmission, transfer, or third-party access to special categories of personal data. No ambiguity.
Specifies concrete deletion triggers: when bias is corrected OR retention period ends, whichever comes first. Clear temporal obligation.
Cross-references GDPR record-keeping obligations and requires inclusion of necessity justification and explanation why other data couldn't achieve the objective. Concrete documentation requirement.

where.text: high-risk AI systems not using techniques involving the training of AI models

chapters.heading: Article 13
chapters.title: Transparency and provision of information to deployers
chapters.fullTitle: Article 13: Transparency and provision of information to deployers
chapters.summaryText: Establishes mandatory transparency requirements for high-risk AI systems. Providers must ensure systems are sufficiently transparent to enable deployers to interpret outputs and use them appropriately. All high-risk AI systems must be accompanied by instructions for use containing specified information about identity, capabilities, limitations, performance metrics, and deployment conditions.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 13 uses prescriptive language ('appropriate type and degree of transparency') without full specification. 'Sufficiently transparent' and 'appropriately' are terms requiring judgment. Concrete specifics are delegated to instructions and implementing acts. Paragraph 3 enumerates required instruction content but implementations may interpret 'relevant', 'accessible' and 'comprehensible' differently across contexts.

Requires 'sufficiently transparent' to enable deployers to 'interpret' and 'use appropriately' — these are contextual standards without fixed metrics. Implementation depends on system type, deployment context, and user sophistication. What constitutes 'appropriate' transparency varies.

Requires information to be 'concise, complete, correct and clear' and 'relevant, accessible and comprehensible' — these are general quality standards without precise measurement criteria. Interpretation depends on deployer knowledge level, system complexity, and use context. Standards development is delegated.

where.text: applicable
applicable
relevant

ambiguitySignal.reason: Lists six categories of required instruction content with significant discretionary application. 'When appropriate', 'where applicable', and 'where relevant' introduce conditional qualification for multiple items (b: 5 conditions, d, e: 3 conditions). Terms like 'performance regarding specific persons or groups' and 'specifications for the input data' lack precise boundaries. Level of technical detail required is not fixed.
ambiguitySignal.delegatedTo: article-40-41 (harmonised standards and common specifications) and article-96 (Commission guidance)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Specific, unambiguous requirement to provide identity and contact details. 'Where applicable' regarding authorised representative is clearly defined in article 22 framework.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Lists seven sub-requirements (i-vii) with multiple conditional qualifiers. 'Known and foreseeable circumstances' is deliberately broad. 'Where applicable' (iv) depends on system technical capabilities. 'When appropriate' (v, vi) requires contextual judgment on whether information is truly material. 'Performance regarding specific persons or groups' lacks clear scope. Data specification requirements are context-dependent. Final phrase (vii) 'enable deployers to interpret and use appropriately' is circular and delegated.
ambiguitySignal.delegatedTo: article-15 (accuracy/robustness standards), article-40-41 (harmonised standards), article-96 (Commission guidance)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Requires disclosure of 'pre-determined' changes and their performance impact 'if any'. The scope of 'pre-determination' and what constitutes a reportable 'change' requires judgment. Not all foreseen changes may be documented at initial conformity assessment. Standard development needed to clarify documentation requirements.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Straightforward cross-reference to article 14 human oversight measures. Scope is defined by article 14 itself. No independent discretion here.

Requires disclosure of 'expected lifetime' and maintenance/care measures with 'necessary' frequency. 'Expected' is predictive and context-dependent. 'Necessary' requires judgment about what maintenance is truly required versus optional. Implementation details depend on system characteristics and deployment environment.

referencesWithContext.snippet: collect, store and interpret the logs in accordance with Article 12

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Where relevant' qualifies the requirement, making it conditional on whether logging is considered relevant to the system. The criteria for relevance are not specified. Description requirements for 'mechanisms' lack precision — the level of technical documentation required depends on system complexity and deployer technical sophistication. Article 12 itself contains discretionary elements on log retention.

chapters.heading: Article 14
chapters.title: Human oversight
chapters.fullTitle: Article 14: Human oversight
chapters.summaryText: Establishes mandatory human oversight requirements for high-risk AI systems. Specifies that systems must be designed for effective human oversight, with measures proportionate to risks and autonomy levels, implemented either by the provider (before market placement) or the deployer. Requires systems to enable natural persons to understand capacities and limitations, detect anomalies, understand automation bias, interpret outputs, override decisions, and intervene safely. For biometric identification systems in Annex III, requires dual verification except in law enforcement contexts where disproportionate.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-14 → deployment obligations for high-risk systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article uses standard regulatory language ('commensurate', 'appropriate', 'as proportionate') that requires contextual interpretation. 'Technically feasible' (para 3) is context-dependent. The scope of 'anomalies, dysfunctions' and 'unexpected performance' (para 4a) allows reasonable variation in implementation. Dual verification exception ('disproportionate') in para 5 delegates proportionality judgment to member state authorities.
Phrase 'effectively overseen' requires judgment on adequacy. 'Appropriate human-machine interface tools' is context-dependent—appropriateness varies by system type, domain, and oversight role.

where.text: such risks persist despite the application of other requirements set out in this Section

when.text: a high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse

ambiguitySignal.reason: Purposive language 'prevent or minimise risks' is intentionally broad. 'Foreseeable misuse' lacks precise boundaries—reasonable disagreement possible on what constitutes 'foreseeable'. No quantitative risk thresholds specified. 'Risks to health, safety or fundamental rights' is non-exhaustive scope.

obligatedActors.source: text (by reference to points a and b)

when.text: technically feasible

ambiguitySignal.reason: Key phrase 'commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use' lacks objective criteria. No methodology specified for assessing what measures are 'commensurate'. 'Technically feasible' is system-specific and time-dependent. Article presents a 'choice of law' model (point a vs b) without clear preference or sequencing guidance.

when.text: technically feasible

ambiguitySignal.reason: Phrase 'measures identified and built...into the high-risk AI system' requires determining what constitutes adequate 'identification' and 'building' of measures. 'Technically feasible' is a context-dependent conditional. Scope of measures not enumerated.

Phrase 'measures...that are appropriate to be implemented by the deployer' delegates appropriateness judgment to deployer without objective criteria. What constitutes 'appropriate' in deployer context depends on specific use case, organizational context, and technical environment. No exhaustive list of measures provided.

obligatedActors.source: text (ensure capacities through provision of system)

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-14-4 (provider/deployer joint obligation on system design and provisioning)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Multiple discretionary terms: 'as appropriate and proportionate' applies to entire paragraph. 'Properly understand', 'duly monitor', 'correctly interpret' lack objective criteria. 'Possible tendency' (automation bias) is subjective. 'Detect and address anomalies, dysfunctions' requires judgment on system behavior classification.

'Properly understand' is subjective. 'Anomalies, dysfunctions and unexpected performance' form a non-exhaustive list. What constitutes 'unexpected' depends on baseline expectation set—reasonable disagreement on classification possible.

obligatedActors.source: text (system design to mitigate automation bias)

applicability.contextNote: especially for systems providing information or recommendations for human decisions

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Possible tendency' is subjective term—no measurable threshold specified. 'Remain aware' is vague on required level of awareness. Implementation varies significantly based on system type and domain. No concrete measures enumerated.

'Correctly interpret' implies objective correctness but interpretation methods may vary by domain/expertise. 'Interpretation tools and methods available' is non-exhaustive and leaves implementation scope open. No specification of accuracy or completeness required.

Obligation is clearly stated: three alternative actions ('decide not to use', 'disregard', 'override or reverse') are explicit and objective. Implementation details may vary, but core obligation is unambiguous.

'Stop button or similar procedure' gives example but allows flexibility. 'Safe state' is context-dependent—what constitutes safety in different application domains varies. For critical systems, definition of safe state requires domain expertise.

What constitutes 'necessary competence, training and authority' for dual verifiers requires implementation guidance.

chapters.heading: Article 15
chapters.title: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity
chapters.fullTitle: Article 15: Accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity
chapters.summaryText: Requires providers of high-risk AI systems to ensure their systems achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity, with consistent performance throughout the system lifecycle. Establishes requirements for measurement methodologies, accuracy declarations, system resilience, and protection against unauthorized alteration or cyber attacks.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 (obligations of providers) → article-15 (specific accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity requirements)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Multiple terms require contextual judgment: 'appropriate level' for accuracy/robustness is not numerically specified; 'consistent performance throughout lifecycle' depends on system-specific interpretation; 'relevant circumstances and risks' for cybersecurity solutions are delegated to implementers. Article 113 references and delegated acts (recital 75) further clarify scope.

where.text: appropriate level

ambiguitySignal.reason: The term 'appropriate level' is intentionally broad and context-dependent. No specific metrics or thresholds are provided in this paragraph; Article 15(3) requires declaration but does not specify measurement methodology here. Article 15(2) delegates to Commission to encourage development of benchmarks.

referencesWithContext.context: spects of how to measure the appropriate levels of accuracy and robustness set out in [paragraph 1](#article-15-1) and any other relevant performance metrics, the Commission shall, in cooperation with
referencesWithContext.snippet: accuracy and robustness set out in paragraph 1 and any other relevant performance

ambiguitySignal.reason: Obligation placed on Commission to 'encourage' development of benchmarks and methodologies 'as appropriate' in cooperation with stakeholders. This is a procedural obligation delegated to Commission discretion and external standard-setting bodies (metrology, benchmarking authorities).

Explicit declaratory obligation: accuracy levels and metrics 'shall be declared' in accompanying instructions. Concrete deliverable with clear location (instructions of use) and content (levels + metrics).

where.text: resilient as possible
technical and organisational measures

ambiguitySignal.reason: Uses qualitative language 'as resilient as possible' and 'where appropriate' which require context-dependent judgment. Specific solutions mentioned (technical redundancy, fail-safe, feedback loop mitigation) are examples but not exhaustive. Implementation depends on system-specific risk assessment and technical feasibility.

where.text: appropriate to relevant circumstances and risks

ambiguitySignal.reason: Three sources of vagueness: (1) 'appropriate to relevant circumstances and risks' is context-dependent with no objective criteria; (2) 'where appropriate' for technical solutions is discretionary; (3) 'include, where appropriate' for specific attack types suggests non-exhaustive examples but does not mandate universal coverage. Implementation depends on threat modeling and risk assessment by provider.

where.text: high-risk AI systems

chapters.summaryText: Establishes comprehensive obligations for providers of high-risk AI systems: ensuring compliance with requirements in Section 2, providing identification and contact information, maintaining a quality management system, documenting the system, keeping automated logs, undergoing conformity assessment, preparing EU declarations of conformity, affixing CE markings, registering systems, taking corrective actions, demonstrating compliance upon authority request, and ensuring accessibility compliance with EU directives.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Core obligations stated with clear modal verbs and specific requirements. However, ambiguity exists in operationalization: compliance with Section 2 requirements requires cross-reference interpretation; conformity assessment procedure and CE marking procedures are delegated to Articles 43 and 48; accessibility requirements incorporate external directives (2016/2102, 2019/882) requiring separate interpretation.

where.text: high-risk AI systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: Twelve specific sub-obligations enumerated with clear structure. Ambiguity derives from delegation to referenced articles (17-20, 43, 47-49) and incorporation of external standards/directives. Terms like 'appropriate' and 'necessary' require contextual judgment in implementation.

Obligation to ensure compliance is clearly stated, but the specific requirements are deferred to Section 2 of the regulation. Full interpretability requires cross-reference to Section 2 (Articles 8-15) which defines concrete technical and governance requirements for high-risk AI systems.

chapters.heading: Article 17
chapters.title: Quality management system
chapters.fullTitle: Article 17: Quality management system
chapters.summaryText: Requires providers of high-risk AI systems to establish and maintain a documented quality management system covering regulatory compliance, design verification, quality control, data management, risk management, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, and accountability. Implementation must be proportionate to the provider's organization size and may be integrated with existing sectoral requirements.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Core obligation with clear mandate ('shall put in place') but significant discretion in implementation scope ('aspects' and 'means to ensure compliance'). Terms like 'systematic and orderly manner' and 'at least' leave room for interpretation on minimum standards. Interpretation of 'proportionate to size' requires organizational assessment.
ambiguitySignal.delegatedTo: implementing acts and harmonised standards (EN standards referenced in Article 40)

where.text: the relevant harmonised standards are not applied in full or do not cover all of the relevant requirements set out in Section 2

ambiguitySignal.reason: The obligation is clear ('shall put in place'), but the substantive scope is large ('shall include at least' with 13 points). The requirement for 'systematic and orderly manner in written policies' is specific, but the adequacy and interrelation of these policies are subject to interpretation. Discretion exists in how each point is implemented for different provider types and system scales.
ambiguitySignal.delegatedTo: harmonised standards (EN standards per Article 40), notified bodies, national competent authorities

ambiguitySignal.reason: Requires a 'strategy for regulatory compliance' but does not specify the minimum content, documentation format, or review frequency. Terms like 'management of modifications' are not defined, leaving discretion on scope (firmware? datasets? parameters?). Harmonised standards will clarify.

Mandates 'techniques, procedures and systematic actions' for design control and verification but does not prescribe specific methodologies. Terms like 'design control' and 'design verification' are not explicitly defined in this context. Scope and rigor will be determined by harmonised standards and notified body guidance.

Requires techniques, procedures and actions for development, quality control and quality assurance, but these terms are not uniformly defined for AI systems. The boundaries between quality control and quality assurance are subject to organizational interpretation. Implementation details are delegated to standards.

Requires examination, test and validation 'before, during and after development' with a frequency statement. The regulation does not specify what constitutes adequate testing, which tests are mandatory, or how 'frequency' should be determined. Interpretation is delegated to harmonised standards and notified body guidance.

Contains a conditional clause: applies when harmonised standards are not fully applied or do not cover all Section 2 requirements. This creates a two-tier system with discretion on: (1) which standards apply, (2) how to assess 'full application', (3) what 'means' are necessary to ensure compliance when standards are insufficient. Implementation will depend on standard development and notified body interpretation.

Lists 14 data operations but does not specify: (1) which operations apply to which systems, (2) minimum quality or governance standards for each operation, (3) retention periods for different data categories, (4) how 'before and for the purpose of placing on market' is assessed. The breadth and open-ended list ('any other operation') creates substantial implementation discretion and will require clarification via standards and guidance.
ambiguitySignal.delegatedTo: harmonised standards, notified bodies, implementing acts on data governance

referencesWithContext.context: - (g) the risk management system referred to in [Article 9](#article-9);
referencesWithContext.snippet: risk management system referred to in Article 9;

ambiguitySignal.reason: Simply incorporates Article 9 risk management requirements by reference. No new ambiguity introduced at this point; ambiguity level is determined by Article 9 itself. Clear linkage and delegation mechanism.

chapters.heading: Article 18
chapters.title: Documentation keeping
chapters.fullTitle: Article 18: Documentation keeping

where.text: for high-risk AI systems

chapters.summaryText: Requires providers to keep specified documentation for high-risk AI systems at the disposal of national competent authorities for 10 years after market placement. Member States may determine alternative conditions when providers cease activity or go bankrupt. Financial institutions subject to sectoral law may integrate documentation with their existing regulatory requirements.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The article clearly specifies documentation types and retention period (10 years). However, 'national competent authorities' scope, practical storage arrangements, and conditions for financial institutions are subject to reasonable interpretation. Article 18(3) allows sectoral integration but defers specifics to Union financial services law.

ambiguitySignal.reason: While the obligation to keep logs is clear ('shall keep'), the phrase 'period appropriate to the intended purpose' is intentionally broad and may be subject to interpretation by different Member States and regulators. The minimum six-month floor provides clarity, but reasonableness of periods beyond that is discretionary.

chapters.summaryText: Establishes provider obligations to take corrective actions when high-risk AI systems are not in conformity with the regulation, including correction, withdrawal, disabling, or recall. Requires providers to inform distributors, deployers, authorised representatives, and importers. Also mandates investigation and reporting to authorities when systems present risks under Article 79(1).

Providers must determine which remedial measure (correction, withdrawal, disable, recall) is suitable based on the specific non-conformity and risk context.

Article 22(3) contains multiple normative tasks with interpretive elements: (1) 'reasoned request' requires judgment about what constitutes adequate reasoning, (2) 'control of the provider' for logs access requires case-by-case assessment, (3) 'applicable' for registration (point e) defers to other legal contexts.

chapters.heading: Article 24
chapters.title: Obligations of distributors
chapters.fullTitle: Article 24: Obligations of distributors
chapters.summaryText: Distributors of high-risk AI systems must verify CE marking, EU declaration of conformity, and instructions for use before making systems available; ensure storage/transport conditions do not compromise compliance; assess non-conformity and take corrective action or inform providers; provide documentation to authorities upon request; and cooperate with market surveillance authorities.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The article uses the standard obligation verb 'shall' but in multiple sub-paragraphs employs evaluative standards such as 'considers or has reason to consider' (24.2, 24.4) which introduce some subjectivity in determining non-conformity. The standard of 'appropriate' storage/transport (24.3) is also context-dependent. The article requires reasonable judgment but clear procedural steps.

The standard is subjective: 'has reason to consider' depends on what would reasonably alert a prudent distributor. The criterion is good-faith assessment but the threshold is not quantified. Additionally, reference to Article 79(1) 'risk' requires interpretation of what constitutes reportable risk.

This is an open standard: what conditions might jeopardise compliance depends on the AI system's technical characteristics, which are not specified here. The distributor must infer appropriate conditions from the system's documentation and operational parameters. The term 'appropriate' is not defined.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The article requires distributors to take corrective action or 'ensure that' others take action. The distributor's obligations when non-conformity involves actors (provider, importer) outside its control is somewhat ambiguous: it must take 'necessary' corrective actions or ensure others do so, but what is 'necessary' depends on the severity and scope of non-conformity. The article also conditions certain notifications on 'immediately inform' when risk presents, which requires rapid assessment.

The scope is defined: 'all information and documentation regarding their actions pursuant to paragraphs 1 to 4 necessary to demonstrate conformity'. The obligation is triggered by clear event (request) and the scope is delineated.

General cooperation obligation triggered by 'relevant competent authorities' taking action on a system a distributor made available. The obligation is clear: cooperate. The scope is 'any action those authorities take' to reduce or mitigate risk, which is inherently adaptive and circumstantial, but the modal duty is unambiguous.

chapters.heading: Article 25
chapters.title: Responsibilities along the AI value chain
chapters.fullTitle: Article 25: Responsibilities along the AI value chain
chapters.summaryText: Article 25 establishes that distributors, importers, deployers, manufacturers, and other third parties can be treated as providers of high-risk AI systems under specific conditions: when they apply their trademark to an existing high-risk AI system, make substantial modifications to maintain high-risk status, or modify intended purpose to create high-risk status. Initial providers retain cooperation obligations while third parties supplying tools and services must specify contractual terms with providers for regulatory compliance.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 25 defines when third parties become providers, but 'substantial modification' and the boundary between modification and intended purpose change requires interpretation against Article 6 criteria.

chapters.heading: Article 26
chapters.title: Obligations of deployers of high-risk AI systems
chapters.fullTitle: Article 26: Obligations of deployers of high-risk AI systems
chapters.summaryText: Establishes core obligations for deployers of high-risk AI systems: ensuring use in accordance with instructions, assigning competent human oversight, monitoring operations, maintaining logs, reporting incidents, and for certain deployers (public authorities, law enforcement), additional requirements including registration, fundamental rights assessments, and special controls on biometric identification systems.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Core deployer obligations are stated clearly but scope of 'appropriate technical and organisational measures' is broad; interpretation depends on specific AI system type and deployment context. 'Without undue delay' in incident reporting is vague without further specification.

The term 'appropriate technical and organisational measures' is open-ended and context-dependent. No specific criteria or thresholds provided for what constitutes 'appropriate' measures for different high-risk AI systems.
Requirements for 'necessary competence, training and authority' are stated but without specific criteria for assessment. 'Necessary support' is not defined and may vary by deployment context.

chapters.title: Fundamental rights impact assessment for high-risk AI systems
chapters.fullTitle: Article 27: Fundamental rights impact assessment for high-risk AI systems
chapters.summaryText: Requires deployers of high-risk AI systems (with exceptions) to conduct and document a fundamental rights impact assessment prior to deployment. Assessment must cover deployer processes, usage frequency, affected persons, risks, human oversight, and risk mitigation measures. Results must be notified to market surveillance authorities using a template developed by the AI Office.

The obligation is clear ('shall designate or establish at least one notifying authority'), but the scope of 'necessary procedures' and the modalities of 'cooperation between notifying authorities' require reasonable interpretation and implementation detail.

No definition or specific criteria are provided in this article for how to assess or measure these conditions. Implementation requires reasonable interpretation and may vary by member state practice.

Whether non-commercial provision of such services is permitted, or what distinguishes 'commercial' from other arrangements, may require clarification.

No specific criteria (e.g., staffing ratios, qualification standards, expertise levels) are provided. Implementation requires judgment and may vary by member state and by AI system complexity.
