chapters.heading: Article 31
chapters.title: Requirements relating to notified bodies
chapters.fullTitle: Article 31: Requirements relating to notified bodies
chapters.summaryText: Establishes comprehensive requirements for notified bodies conducting conformity assessments of high-risk AI systems. Mandates organizational structure, quality management, independence, impartiality, confidentiality, liability insurance, professional competence, and participation in coordination activities and standardization.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article establishes clear obligations but uses terms that require interpretation: 'suitable cybersecurity requirements' (1.2), 'confidence in their performance' (1.3), 'necessary for operations' exception (1.4), 'consultancy services' scope (1.5), 'appropriate liability insurance' (1.9), 'highest degree of professional integrity' (1.10), 'sufficient internal competences' (1.11). Several terms delegate specificity to Member State implementation and standards.
Straightforward establishment requirement. Mandatory ('shall'), subject matter unambiguous, conditions fully specified.

'Suitable cybersecurity requirements' is undefined and delegated to standards development. 'Resources and process requirements necessary to fulfil their tasks' lacks specific quantification or scope definition.
'Ensure confidence in their performance' is a subjective standard without objective criteria. What constitutes sufficient 'confidence' is not defined and requires judgment calls.

referencesWithContext.context: provider of a high-risk AI system in relation to which they perform conformity assessment activities
referencesWithContext.snippet: notified bodies perform conformity assessment

unless.text: the use of assessed high-risk AI systems is necessary for the operations of the conformity assessment body, or for personal purposes

ambiguitySignal.reason: Prohibition clear but exceptions introduce interpretive elements: 'necessary for operations' and 'personal purposes' lack precise scope. 'Economic interest' and 'competitors' require case-by-case assessment.

Prohibition uses qualitative standard: 'any activity that might conflict with their independence of judgement or integrity' relies on judgment. Scope of 'consultancy services' and what constitutes impermissible activities requires interpretation.

Obligation to 'safeguard independence, objectivity and impartiality' and 'promote and apply principles' is aspirational without specific implementation criteria. What constitutes adequate safeguards is undefined and subject to interpretation.

referencesWithContext.context: maintain, in accordance with Article 78, the confidentiality of the information which comes into their possession during the performance of conformity assessment activities
referencesWithContext.snippet: maintain, in accordance with Article 78, the confidentiality

when.text: its disclosure is required by law

exception.text: except in relation to the notifying authorities of the Member State in which their activities are carried out

ambiguitySignal.reason: Clear obligation with well-defined conditions and exceptions. Exception for disclosure 'when required by law' is standard legal language. Reference to Article 78 clarifies confidentiality standards.

Obligation to 'take due account' of multiple factors (size, sector, structure, complexity) lacks objective criteria. 'Due account' is subjective and context-dependent, requiring judgment on proportionality and appropriateness.

unless.text: liability is assumed by the Member State in which they are established in accordance with national law or that Member State is itself directly responsible for the conformity assessment

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Appropriate liability insurance' lacks specific criteria: coverage amounts, types, conditions are not defined. 'Unless liability is assumed by the Member State' introduces broad exception that depends on Member State determination.

'Highest degree of professional integrity' is qualitative and aspirational without objective measurement criteria. 'Requisite competence in the specific field' is undefined and subject to determination on a case-by-case basis.

'Sufficient internal competences' and 'permanent availability' lack quantified standards. What constitutes 'sufficient' experience and knowledge for 'relevant types of AI systems' is undefined and context-dependent. Breadth of relevant types not specified.

referencesWithContext.context: Notified bodies shall participate in coordination activities as referred to in Article 38. They shall also take part directly, or be represented in, European standardisation organisations
referencesWithContext.snippet: participate in coordination activities as referred to in Article 38

ambiguitySignal.reason: Obligations are clear but level of participation and representation ('directly, or be represented in') and meaning of being 'aware and up to date in respect of relevant standards' are subject to interpretation based on practical circumstances.

chapters.heading: Article 32
chapters.title: Presumption of conformity with requirements relating to notified bodies
chapters.fullTitle: Article 32: Presumption of conformity with requirements relating to notified bodies
chapters.summaryText: Establishes a rebuttable presumption that conformity assessment bodies meeting criteria in relevant harmonised standards are presumed to comply with Article 31 requirements, to the extent those standards cover the applicable requirements for notified bodies.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article-level declaratory text. Establishes a presumption with clear conditions: demonstration of conformity with harmonised standards. No discretionary terms, scope depends on standards coverage but presumption itself is clear.

referencesWithContext.context: of the European Union, it shall be presumed to comply with the requirements set out in [Article 31](#article-31) in so far as the applicable harmonised standards cover those requirements.
referencesWithContext.snippet: ply with the requirements set out in Article 31 in so far as the applicable harmonise

where.text: a conformity assessment body demonstrates its conformity with the criteria laid down in the relevant harmonised standards or parts thereof, the references of which have been published in the Official Journal of the European Union

unless.text: the applicable harmonised standards do not cover the requirements set out in Article 31

penaltyExposure.alternativeFormula: 3% of total worldwide annual turnover for the preceding financial year
penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-99-4-f (notified bodies obligations) → article-31 (which this article qualifies) → article-32-1

ambiguitySignal.reason: Establishes presumption conditioned on harmonised standards compliance. Ambiguity arises from: (1) scope depends on which harmonised standards are published; (2) 'in so far as' creates interpretive boundary on coverage; (3) technical assessment of conformity with harmonised standards involves expert judgment. However, the presumption mechanism itself is clear.

chapters.heading: Article 33
chapters.title: Subsidiaries of notified bodies and subcontracting
chapters.fullTitle: Article 33: Subsidiaries of notified bodies and subcontracting

chapters.summaryText: Article 33 requires notified bodies to ensure their subcontractors and subsidiaries meet independence and professional requirements, and to assume full responsibility for their work. Subcontracting requires provider consent and documented subsidiaries must be publicly listed.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The requirement for subcontractors/subsidiaries to 'meet requirements in Article 31' is clear, but the scope of 'meets requirements' could involve subjective assessment of independence, impartiality and competence. The term 'full responsibility' in paragraph 2 could be interpreted broadly.

referencesWithContext.context: Notified body subcontractors and subsidiaries must meet requirements laid down in Article 31
referencesWithContext.snippet: shall ensure that the subcontractor or the subsidiary meets the requirements laid down in [Article 31]

where.text: a notified body subcontracts specific tasks connected with the conformity assessment or has recourse to a subsidiary

ambiguitySignal.reason: The requirement is explicit: notified bodies must ensure subcontractors/subsidiaries meet Article 31 requirements and must inform the notifying authority. Both conditions are clearly stated with mandatory language (shall).

where.text: when tasks are performed by subcontractors or subsidiaries

ambiguitySignal.reason: While the requirement to take 'full responsibility' is clear, the scope and extent of that responsibility could be subject to interpretation. The regulation does not define the limits of 'full responsibility' or the circumstances under which a notified body could claim to have met this obligation.

where.text: when activities are to be subcontracted or carried out by a subsidiary

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 3 uses 'may' indicating permission, but also imposes a mandatory requirement for notified bodies to 'make a list of their subsidiaries publicly available'. The boundary between the permission and the obligation could be subject to interpretation regarding when the public availability requirement applies.

where.text: when assessment documents of subcontractors or subsidiaries are created under this Regulation

ambiguitySignal.reason: The requirement is specific and explicit: documents concerning assessment of subcontractor/subsidiary qualifications and work must be kept at the disposal of the notifying authority for five years from termination. The timeline and custodian are clearly defined.

chapters.heading: Article 34
chapters.title: Operational obligations of notified bodies
chapters.fullTitle: Article 34: Operational obligations of notified bodies
chapters.summaryText: Notified bodies must verify the conformity of high-risk AI systems according to specified procedures, minimize unnecessary burdens on providers (especially micro and small enterprises), and make documentation available to notifying authorities for assessment and monitoring.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The requirement to 'avoid unnecessary burdens' is context-dependent and requires interpretation based on provider size, sector, and AI system complexity. The standard of 'due account' invokes subjective judgment.

referencesWithContext.context: igh-risk AI systems in accordance with the conformity assessment procedures set out in [Article 43](#article-43).
referencesWithContext.snippet: ity assessment procedures set out in Article 43.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The obligation is explicitly stated with a clear modal verb ('shall'), specific actor (notified bodies), specific action (verify conformity), and precise reference to conformity assessment procedures in Article 43.

when.text: performing their activities

considerations: degree of complexity of the high-risk AI system

ambiguitySignal.reason: The standard 'avoid unnecessary burdens' is inherently vague and subjective. The requirement to 'take due account' of multiple contextual factors (size, sector, structure, complexity) lacks objective criteria. The balancing requirement between minimizing burden and respecting 'degree of rigour' is inherently discretionary.

referencesWithContext.context: ion, including the providers' documentation, to the notifying authority referred to in [Article 28](#article-28) to allow that authority to conduct its assessment, designation, notification and monitoring activities
referencesWithContext.snippet: notifying authority referred to in Article 28 to allow that authority to conduct its assessment

ambiguitySignal.reason: While the obligation to submit documentation 'upon request' is clear, determining what constitutes 'all relevant documentation' may involve interpretation. The phrase 'facilitate the assessment outlined in this Section' references procedural context that requires understanding of broader section requirements.

chapters.heading: Article 35
chapters.title: Identification numbers and lists of notified bodies
chapters.fullTitle: Article 35: Identification numbers and lists of notified bodies
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the Commission's obligations to assign unique identification numbers to each notified body and maintain a publicly available, up-to-date list of notified bodies with their identification numbers and authorized activities.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Two mandatory duties with unambiguous language: (1) assign single ID numbers to notified bodies, (2) maintain and publish a list. No discretionary terms, no conditional scope, no delegation to other acts.

where.text: a body is notified under more than one Union act

ambiguitySignal.reason: Mandatory obligation to assign a 'single identification number' to each notified body. Condition is explicit and unambiguous: applies 'even where a body is notified under more than one Union act'. No discretionary language.

Two clearly stated obligations: (1) make list publicly available, including identification numbers and authorized activities, (2) ensure list is kept up to date. 'Publicly available' is standard terminology. 'Up to date' is unambiguous in context of list maintenance.

chapters.heading: Article 36
chapters.title: Changes to notifications
chapters.fullTitle: Article 36: Changes to notifications
chapters.summaryText: Specifies procedures for managing changes to notified body notifications. Establishes rules for notification updates, conditions for certificate validity during suspension/withdrawal, and requirements for coordinating with other Member States and the Commission. Article 36 is critical to notification integrity and market surveillance.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Multiple conditional provisions with discretionary terms such as 'appropriate steps', 'without delay', 'as soon as possible', and 'timely'. Temporal ambiguity in paragraph 3 ('one year before' cessation) and specification requirements in paragraphs 7-9 allowing Member State discretion in implementation.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 36 is part of Chapter III Section 4 and applies from 2025-08-02 per Article 113(2).

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Immediate investigation required upon finding 'sufficient reason'. No specific deadline for completion or decision on restriction/suspension/withdrawal.

Terms like 'reasonable period' (7c) and 'appropriate measures' (7e) lack specificity. Delegation to Member State for risk determinations.

Terms such as 'confirmed no risk' and 'assumed responsibility' involve Member State/authority discretion. Notification timing to other bodies is subject to interpretation ('immediately').

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Upon withdrawal: 9-month provisional validity period under conditions 9a and 9b. National authority may extend for additional 3-month periods (max 12 months total). Assessment completion by replacement body: within 12 months of withdrawal. Notification timing: 'immediately'.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Framework article establishing Commission oversight and member state remediation procedures for notified body competence. Operative conditions clearly specified (doubt about competence), actors identified (Commission, notifying authority, member state), and remedial procedures outlined.

chapters.summaryText: Permits conformity assessment bodies established in third countries with which the Union has concluded an agreement to operate as notified bodies under this Regulation, provided they meet the requirements of Article 31 or ensure equivalent compliance.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article establishes a clear permission with stated conditions: bodies from third countries may be authorized only if they meet Article 31 requirements or provide equivalent compliance. Condition is specific and verifiable.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The phrase 'ensure an equivalent level of compliance' allows reasonable discretion in interpretation. What constitutes 'equivalent' is not quantified and may require case-by-case assessment. This aligns with harmonized technical standards but creates room for differing interpretations across Member States.

chapters.heading: Section 5
chapters.title: Standards, conformity assessment, certificates, registration
chapters.fullTitle: Section 5: Standards, conformity assessment, certificates, registration

chapters.heading: Article 40
chapters.title: Harmonised standards and standardisation deliverables
chapters.fullTitle: Article 40: Harmonised standards and standardisation deliverables
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the legal framework for harmonised standards and standardisation deliverables under the AI Act. Creates a presumption of conformity for high-risk AI systems and general-purpose AI models that comply with published harmonised standards. Mandates the Commission to issue standardisation requests covering all requirements in Chapter III Section 2 and Chapter V Sections 2 and 3. Requires standardisation participants to promote investment, innovation, global cooperation, and multi-stakeholder governance.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 40 delegates the specification of conformity criteria to harmonised standards developed by European standardisation organisations under Regulation 1025/2012. The standards themselves (not yet published) will define what constitutes compliance with high-risk AI requirements. The Commission's standardisation request and subsequent standard-development process determine the actual compliance obligations. Paragraph 3 uses vague language ('best efforts', 'adequate representation') without measurable thresholds.

referencesWithContext.context: out in Section 2 of this Chapter or, as applicable, with the obligations set out in of [Chapter V](#chapter-v), Sections 2 and 3, of this Regulation, to the extent that those standards cover those re

ambiguitySignal.reason: The presumption of conformity is conditional on harmonised standards being published in the Official Journal. Until such standards exist and are published, providers cannot rely on this conformity pathway. The phrase 'to the extent that those standards cover those requirements' creates scope ambiguity: if a standard only partially covers requirements, the provider must demonstrate compliance with uncovered requirements through alternative means (common specifications or direct compliance demonstration).

referencesWithContext.snippet: 2. In accordance with Article 10 of Regulation (EU) No 1025/2012, the Comm

when.text: preparing a standardisation request
issuing a standardisation request to European standardisation organisations

procedural_requirements.text: consult the Board and relevant stakeholders, including the advisory forum
specify that standards have to be clear, consistent, and aiming to ensure that high-risk AI systems or general-purpose AI models meet the relevant requirements
request European standardisation organisations to provide evidence of their best efforts to fulfil the objectives

ambiguitySignal.reason: The Commission must issue standardisation requests 'without undue delay' — this temporal requirement is undefined and non-justiciable. 'Best efforts' (third subparagraph) is a vague standard with no measurable threshold or enforcement mechanism. The standards must be 'clear' and 'consistent' but these are qualitative assessments. The consultation requirement ('shall consult the Board and relevant stakeholders') does not specify how input must be considered or what weight it carries.

Participants 'shall seek to promote' is aspirational language with no measurable compliance threshold. The objectives are broadly stated: 'investment and innovation', 'competitiveness and growth', 'strengthen global cooperation', 'enhance multi-stakeholder governance'. None of these have defined success criteria or enforcement mechanisms. The reference to 'existing international standards... consistent with Union values, fundamental rights and interests' requires subjective assessment. 'Balanced representation' and 'effective participation' (cross-reference to Articles 5, 6, 7 of Regulation 1025/2012) are process requirements without clear quantitative or qualitative thresholds.

chapters.heading: Article 42
chapters.title: Presumption of conformity with certain requirements
chapters.fullTitle: Article 42: Presumption of conformity with certain requirements
chapters.summaryText: Establishes two safe harbor provisions: high-risk AI systems trained and tested on data reflecting their intended setting are presumed to comply with data governance requirements; systems certified under cybersecurity schemes are presumed to comply with cybersecurity requirements to the extent covered by the certification.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article-level node establishing presumptions of conformity. The presumptions are clearly stated and limited to specific conditions (data reflecting intended setting, cybersecurity certification coverage).

referencesWithContext.context: to be used shall be presumed to comply with the relevant requirements laid down in [Article 10 (4)](#article-10-4).
referencesWithContext.snippet: levant requirements laid down in Article 10 (4).

ambiguitySignal.reason: The condition 'data reflecting the specific geographical, behavioural, contextual or functional setting' is stated but requires interpretation regarding what constitutes adequate reflection and coverage of these settings. The four setting types are enumerated but their boundaries and assessment criteria are not fully specified.

referencesWithContext.context: opean Union shall be presumed to comply with the cybersecurity requirements set out in [Article 15](#article-15) of this Regulation in so far as the cybersecurity certificate or statement of conformit
referencesWithContext.snippet: ybersecurity requirements set out in Article 15 of this Regulation in so far as the c

conditions: High-risk AI system has been certified or received a statement of conformity under a cybersecurity scheme pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2019/881
References of the scheme have been published in the Official Journal of the European Union
Cybersecurity certificate or statement of conformity covers the requirements in Article 15

ambiguitySignal.reason: Establishes a limited safe harbor for cybersecurity compliance. The conditions are clearly stated: certification under Regulation (EU) 2019/881, publication in Official Journal, and coverage determination limited to 'in so far as' the certificate covers Article 15 requirements. The scope limitation is explicit.

chapters.title: Conformity assessment
chapters.fullTitle: Article 43: Conformity assessment
chapters.summaryText: Defines the conformity assessment procedures that providers of high-risk AI systems must follow to demonstrate compliance, with different procedures depending on the type of high-risk system and whether harmonised standards have been applied. Grants the Commission power to amend these procedures via delegated acts.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The conformity assessment procedures are defined by detailed technical requirements in Annexes VI and VII, which are subject to future amendment by Commission delegated acts. The exact procedures vary based on system classification and whether standards have been applied.

chapters.heading: Article 44
chapters.title: Certificates
chapters.fullTitle: Article 44: Certificates

referencesWithContext.context: Reference context from graph metrics

chapters.summaryText: Establishes requirements for certificates issued by notified bodies for high-risk AI systems. Specifies that certificates must be drawn up in a language understood by relevant authorities, sets validity periods (up to 5 years for Annex I systems, 4 years for Annex III), permits extensions upon re-assessment, and authorizes suspension or withdrawal when systems no longer meet requirements unless corrected by the provider within an appropriate deadline.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-f → Article 31, 33, 34 (notified body obligations) → Article 44 (certificate requirements as part of notified body obligations)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Mixed obligation types across paragraphs. Paragraph 1 clearly mandates language requirement. Paragraph 2 uses 'shall not exceed' (potentially a ceiling rather than a directive to limit). Paragraph 3 condition 'unless compliance...ensured' creates complexity regarding when suspension/withdrawal becomes mandatory vs. discretionary. 'Proportionality principle' and 'appropriate deadline' require subjective judgment.

referencesWithContext.context: Certificates issued by notified bodies in accordance with [Annex VII](#annex-vii) shall be drawn-up in a language which can be easily understood by the relevant authorities in the Member State in which the notified body is established.
referencesWithContext.snippet: by notified bodies in accordance with Annex VII shall be drawn-up in a language which

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-f → notified body obligations → article-44-1 (language requirement)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Mandatory requirement with simple scope: certificates must be in a language understood by relevant authorities where the notified body is established. No conditions, exceptions, or discretionary elements.

referencesWithContext.context: for the period they indicate, which shall not exceed five years for AI systems covered by [Annex I](#annex-i), and four years for AI systems covered by [Annex III](#annex-iii). At the request of the provider, the validity of a certificate may be extended for further periods, each not exceeding five years for AI systems covered by [Annex I](#annex-i), and four years for AI systems covered by [Annex III](#annex-iii), based on a re-assessment in accordance with the applicable conformity assessment procedures.
referencesWithContext.snippet: for AI systems covered by Annex I, and four years for AI systems covered by Annex III

referencesWithContext.context: for AI systems covered by [Annex I](#annex-i), and four years for AI systems covered by [Annex III](#annex-iii). At the request of the provider, the validity of a certificate may be extended for further periods, each not exceeding five years for AI systems covered by [Annex I](#annex-i), and four years for AI systems covered by [Annex III](#annex-iii), based on a re-assessment in accordance with the applicable conformity assessment procedures.
referencesWithContext.snippet: for AI systems covered by Annex III. At the request of the provider, the validity of a certificate may be extended for further periods

where.text: a certificate is issued

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-f → notified body obligations → article-44-2 (certificate validity requirements)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Multiple elements create interpretive complexity: (1) 'shall not exceed' is phrased as a ceiling but functions as a mandatory constraint on validity period; (2) extension is permissive ('may be extended') but must be based on re-assessment per conformity assessment procedures (which are themselves complex); (3) 'supplement to certificate' validity rules require interpretation of what constitutes a supplement; (4) 'applicable conformity assessment procedures' is delegated reference.
ambiguitySignal.delegatedTo: annex-i, annex-iii, applicable conformity assessment procedures

where.text: a notified body finds that an AI system no longer meets the requirements set out in Section 2

unless.text: compliance with those requirements is ensured by appropriate corrective action taken by the provider of the system within an appropriate deadline set by the notified body

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-f → notified body obligations → article-44-3 (certificate suspension/withdrawal procedures)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Significant discretionary and interpretive elements: (1) 'appropriate corrective action' and 'appropriate deadline' are undefined and require subjective judgment; (2) 'proportionality principle' is invoked but not defined; (3) conditions for when suspension/withdrawal 'shall' occur vs. when it 'may' occur depend on notified body assessment of appropriateness of corrective action; (4) appeal procedure details are referenced but not specified in this article; (5) 'taking account of' proportionality is hortatory, not mandatory.
ambiguitySignal.delegatedTo: notified body (discretion on proportionality, corrective action adequacy, deadline setting)

chapters.heading: Article 46
chapters.title: Derogation from conformity assessment procedure
chapters.fullTitle: Article 46: Derogation from conformity assessment procedure
chapters.summaryText: Article 46 permits market surveillance authorities to authorize placement or putting into service of specific high-risk AI systems without the normal conformity assessment, subject to strict conditions, notification requirements, and review by the Commission and other Member States.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Article 99 does not list Article 46 obligations in penalty tiers; indirect penalties only if operators violate substantive requirements referenced (Article 43, Section 2 requirements).

ambiguitySignal.reason: Terms like 'exceptional reasons of public security', 'protection of key industrial and infrastructural assets', and 'without undue delay' require Member State judgment. The scope of 'essential' standards for law-enforcement derogation is context-dependent.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 113(2) applies Chapter III Section 3 (which includes Article 46) from 2026-08-02.

referencesWithContext.context: By way of derogation from [Article 43](#article-43) and upon a duly justified request, any market surveillance authority may authorise the placing on the market or the putting into service of specific high-risk AI systems
referencesWithContext.snippet: derogation from Article 43

chapters.summaryText: Market surveillance authorities may authorize high-risk AI systems to be placed on the market or put into service without normal conformity assessment, upon duly justified request, for limited periods and specified exceptional reasons.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Procedural provision establishing authority power; not directly subject to Article 99 penalties.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Scope of 'exceptional reasons' is broadly stated; implementation guidance needed on what constitutes 'duly justified request', 'limited period', and 'without undue delay'.

referencesWithContext.context: may put a specific high-risk AI system into service without the authorisation referred to in [paragraph 1](#article-46-1), provided that such authorisation is requested during or after the use
referencesWithContext.snippet: without the authorisation referred to in paragraph 1

only_if.text: duly justified situation of urgency for exceptional reasons of public security or specific, substantial and imminent threat to life or physical safety

provided_that.text: authorization is requested during or after the use without undue delay

chapters.summaryText: Law-enforcement and civil protection authorities may put specific high-risk AI systems into service without prior authorization in urgent situations involving public security or imminent threats to life, provided authorization is sought promptly thereafter.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Emergency exception provision; operator penalties apply only if results/outputs not immediately discarded post-refusal.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Scope of 'specific, substantial and imminent threat' and 'without undue delay' are context-dependent and may differ across Member States' law enforcement practices.

referencesWithContext.context: The authorisation referred to in [paragraph 1](#article-46-1) shall be issued only if the market surveillance authority concludes that the high-risk AI system complies with the requirements of Section 2.

chapters.summaryText: Authorizations may only be issued if the market surveillance authority confirms compliance with Section 2 requirements. The issuing authority must notify the Commission and other Member States, except where sensitive law-enforcement operational data is involved.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Procedural/authorization requirement; penalties apply if underlying Section 2 requirements violated, not the notification itself.

ambiguitySignal.reason: What constitutes 'sensitive operational data' and the precise scope of Section 2 compliance requirements may require clarification through Member State cooperation or regulatory guidance.

referencesWithContext.snippet: in accordance with paragraph 1

chapters.summaryText: If no objections are raised within 15 calendar days of the notification by either a Member State or the Commission, the authorization is deemed justified and becomes effective.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Procedural timeline provision; no direct penalty exposure under Article 99.

ambiguitySignal.reason: 15-day timeline and silence-as-consent mechanism are explicit and unambiguous procedural requirements.

address decision to Member State and relevant operators

chapters.summaryText: Where objections are raised or the Commission identifies legal concerns, the Commission must conduct without-delay consultations with the Member State and operators, then decide whether the authorization is justified and formally communicate the decision.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Procedural obligation for Commission to resolve disputes; no direct penalty under Article 99.

ambiguitySignal.reason: What constitutes 'without delay' in Commission decision-making and the standard of 'contrary to Union law' or 'unfounded' compliance findings may require clarification through precedent.

where.text: the Commission considers the authorisation unjustified

chapters.summaryText: When the Commission finds an authorization unjustified, the market surveillance authority of the Member State must withdraw the authorization.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Remedial obligation; failure to withdraw may expose Member State to enforcement action, not direct penalty under Article 99.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Withdrawal mechanism is explicit and unambiguous once Commission determination of unjustification is made.

chapters.summaryText: For high-risk AI systems related to regulated products (listed in Annex I Section A), only derogations from conformity assessment that are already established in the applicable Union harmonisation legislation apply; Article 46 does not expand derogations beyond those existing frameworks.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Scope-limiting provision; ensures consistent application with existing harmonisation legislation derogations.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Determining whether a specific high-risk AI system is 'related to' a regulated product requires interpretation of Annex I Section A scope and may need clarification through regulatory guidance.

chapters.heading: Article 47
chapters.title: EU declaration of conformity
chapters.fullTitle: Article 47: EU declaration of conformity
chapters.summaryText: Establishes requirements for an EU declaration of conformity for high-risk AI systems, including content specifications, language requirements, recordkeeping obligations, and Commission authority to update requirements via delegated acts.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 (obligations of providers) → this node

ambiguitySignal.reason: The article specifies concrete requirements for the declaration (content, language, recordkeeping period of 10 years). The conditions are explicit (for each high-risk AI system placed on market or put into service). Language such as 'shall draw up', 'shall keep', 'shall be translated' are unambiguous mandatory directives.

where.text: for each high-risk AI system
after the high-risk AI system has been placed on the market or put into service

ambiguitySignal.reason: Specific obligations stated with precise requirements: written format, machine readable, physical or electronically signed, kept for 10 years, submitted upon request. No discretionary language or delegation.

referencesWithContext.snippet: hall contain the information set out in Annex V, and shall be translated into a language

ambiguitySignal.reason: The phrase 'language that can be easily understood' in the translation requirement is somewhat subjective. What constitutes 'easy understanding' may vary by interpretation, though the intent to provide accessible translations is clear. The reference to Annex V requires checking that document for specifics on required content.

where.text: high-risk AI systems are subject to other Union harmonisation legislation which also requires an EU declaration of conformity

ambiguitySignal.reason: The condition is explicit (where other Union harmonisation legislation also requires an EU declaration). The obligation is clear: draw up a single declaration that meets all applicable requirements. The directive to identify the applicable legislation is specific.

The phrase 'keep the EU declaration of conformity up-to-date as appropriate' introduces discretion in determining what 'as appropriate' means. The circumstances that trigger an update obligation are not explicitly enumerated, requiring interpretation of what changes necessitate an update.

chapters.heading: Article 48
chapters.title: CE marking
chapters.fullTitle: Article 48: CE marking

referencesWithContext.context: principles set out in Article 30 of Regulation (EC) No 765/2008
referencesWithContext.snippet: subject to the general principles set out in Article 30 of Regulation (EC)

chapters.summaryText: Mandates CE marking for high-risk AI systems according to EU Regulation 765/2008 principles, with specific requirements for digital, visible, and legible affixing, and inclusion of notified body identification numbers where applicable.

ambiguitySignal.reason: CE marking requirements are mostly clear, but paragraph 3's exception clause ('where that is not possible or not warranted on account of the nature of the high-risk AI system') grants discretion to providers in determining applicability. Paragraph 4's 'where applicable' creates additional conditional scope.

referencesWithContext.context: general principles set out in Article 30 of Regulation (EC) No 765/2008
referencesWithContext.snippet: subject to the general principles set out in Article 30

ambiguitySignal.reason: References external EU regulation framework. Clear obligation to comply with established CE marking principles. No discretionary language.

only_if.text: it can easily be accessed via the interface from which that system is accessed or via an easily accessible machine-readable code or other electronic means

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Easily accessible' is a qualitative standard that could be interpreted differently by providers and enforcement authorities. 'Machine-readable code or other electronic means' leaves implementation choices open.

where.text: that is not possible or not warranted on account of the nature of the high-risk AI system

ambiguitySignal.reason: The exception 'not warranted on account of the nature of the high-risk AI system' grants significant discretion to providers. Determining what constitutes 'not possible or not warranted' is subjective and lacks objective criteria.

referencesWithContext.context: identification number of the notified body responsible for the conformity assessment procedures set out in [Article 43](#article-43)

chapters.heading: Article 49
chapters.title: Registration
chapters.fullTitle: Article 49: Registration
chapters.summaryText: Article 49 mandates registration of high-risk AI systems in an EU database. Providers must register systems listed in Annex III before placing them on the market or putting into service, except for certain systems covered under Article 6(3). Deployers of certain high-risk systems must also register their use. For sensitive AI systems in law enforcement and border control, registration occurs in a secure non-public section of the database. National-level registration applies to specific high-risk systems designated in Annex III, point 2.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The precise scope of 'registration' obligations and what constitutes adequate information for the database require interpretation in context with Annexes VIII and IX. The distinction between secure non-public sections (para 4) and public sections involves delegated determination by the Commission.

chapters.heading: Article 50
chapters.title: Transparency obligations for providers and deployers of certain AI systems
chapters.fullTitle: Article 50: Transparency obligations for providers and deployers of certain AI systems
chapters.summaryText: Imposes transparency requirements on providers and deployers concerning direct human interaction, synthetic content generation, emotion/biometric recognition, and deepfakes. Includes disclosure obligations, labeling requirements in machine-readable formats, and carve-outs for law enforcement and editorial content. Delegates code-of-practice implementation to the AI Office and Commission.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-g → article-50 (transparency obligations for providers and deployers)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Key concepts require interpretation: 'reasonably well-informed' (para 1), 'technically feasible' and 'generally acknowledged state of art' (para 2), 'appropriate manner' for artistic content (para 4), 'matters of public interest' (para 4). Implementation details of machine-readable marking delegated to codes of practice.

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect' natural person is a contextual standard requiring judgment; applicability depends on circumstances and context of use which are case-specific. Law enforcement exception scope depends on national laws.

authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4-g → article-50-2 (transparency obligation regarding synthetic content)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Precise technical specifications for machine-readable marking delegated to 'relevant technical standards'. Feasibility assessment ('technically feasible', 'generally acknowledged state of art', 'specificities and limitations of various types of content', 'costs of implementation') requires ongoing technical judgment and standardization work.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Obligation is clearly stated: inform natural persons and ensure GDPR/GDPR compliance. Exception for law enforcement is explicitly listed with appropriate safeguards requirement. References to external Union data protection law (GDPR, national law) provide sufficient specificity.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Several subjective standards: 'evidently artistic, creative, satirical, fictional or analogous work' requires judgment on intent and obviousness; 'matters of public interest' (text) is context-dependent; 'appropriate manner that does not hamper the display or enjoyment of the work' (artistic content) balances disclosure against user experience without prescriptive guidance.

chapters.fullTitle: Article 51: Classification of general-purpose AI models as general-purpose AI models with systemic risk
chapters.summaryText: Establishes criteria for classifying general-purpose AI models as having systemic risk based on high-impact capabilities or Commission designation. Includes a computational threshold presumption and authorizes the Commission to adopt delegated acts to update thresholds as technology evolves.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 (provider obligations Article 16 et al.) → applicable as a provider obligation for compliance with classification and notification procedures in Articles 51-52

Paragraph 1 provides two independent classification pathways: (a) technical evaluation and (b) Commission discretion. The interplay between these conditions and how they affect provider obligations in Articles 52+ requires careful interpretation.
The phrase 'appropriate technical tools and methodologies' and 'high impact capabilities' are not defined in this article. These concepts are delegated to Annex XIII and implementing standards.

chapters.title: Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models

chapters.heading: Article 53
chapters.title: Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models
chapters.fullTitle: Article 53: Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models
chapters.summaryText: Establishes core obligations for general-purpose AI model providers: maintain technical documentation per Annex XI, provide model information to downstream integrators per Annex XII, implement copyright compliance policies, and publish summaries of training data. Includes exemptions for free/open-source models and provisions for delegated acts on methodologies and annex amendments.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 53(1) imposes clear obligations but the scope of 'technical documentation' and 'information about content used for training' requires reference to Annexes XI and XII for full specification. Article 53(4) creates a compliance pathway via codes of practice or harmonised standards, introducing discretion in how providers demonstrate compliance. The treatment of trade secrets and IP rights (Article 53(1)(b)) introduces conditional language requiring balancing of competing interests.

without_prejudice_to.text: the need to observe and protect intellectual property rights and confidential business information or trade secrets in accordance with Union and national law

ambiguitySignal.reason: The paragraph contains four distinct obligations with varying specificity. Points (a)–(d) define what documentation shall be prepared and disclosed, but the exact scope of 'capabilities and limitations' (1-b-i) and what constitutes an 'understanding' thereof is not fully specified. Point (c) requires 'state-of-the-art' copyright compliance technology—a vague reference to evolving technology standards.

referencesWithContext.context: results of its evaluation, which shall contain, at a minimum, the information set out in [Annex XI](#annex-xi) for the purpose of providing it, upon request, to the AI Office and the national competent authorities
referencesWithContext.snippet: a minimum, the information set out in Annex XI for the purpose of providing it, upon request

ambiguitySignal.reason: The specific content and format of technical documentation is delegated to Annex XI. The exact scope of what constitutes proper 'training and testing process' results is subject to interpretation based on annex specifications and may evolve with sector practice.

referencesWithContext.context: draw up, keep up-to-date and make available information and documentation to providers of AI systems who intend to integrate the general-purpose AI model into their AI systems. Without prejudice to the need to observe and protect intellectual property rights and confidential business information or trade secrets in accordance with Union and national law, the information and documentation shall contain, at a minimum, the elements set out in [Annex XII](#annex-xii)
referencesWithContext.snippet: elements set out in Annex XII

without_prejudice_to.text: the need to observe and protect intellectual property rights and confidential business information or trade secrets in accordance with Union and national law

ambiguitySignal.reason: The obligation requires enabling downstream AI system providers to have 'a good understanding' of model capabilities and limitations—'good understanding' is subjective and may differ between providers and authorities. The sub-points (i)–(ii) require 'compliance' with regulations and contain minimum element requirements delegated to Annex XII. The balancing of IP protection with transparency creates interpretive tension.

Requires implementing a policy to comply with copyright law 'in particular' to identify and comply with rights reservations expressed under Directive 2019/790 Article 4(3), using 'state-of-the-art technologies.' Neither 'state-of-the-art' nor the practical scope of identifying such reservations is clearly defined. This is a capabilities-based obligation without precise, measurable criteria.

The obligation to publish a 'sufficiently detailed summary' of training content relies on a template provided by the AI Office. The threshold of 'sufficiently detailed' is not defined in the article but deferred to the template. This creates flexibility but introduces ambiguity pending template publication.

referencesWithContext.context: The obligations set out in [paragraph 1](#article-53-1), points (a) and (b), shall not apply to providers of AI models that are released under a free and open-source licence
referencesWithContext.snippet: obligations set out in paragraph 1, points (a) and (b), shall not apply

ambiguitySignal.reason: The exemption applies to models 'released under a free and open-source licence that allows for the access, usage, modification, and distribution'—the definition of qualifying licences is not exhaustively specified. However, the exception is negated for 'general-purpose AI models with systemic risks,' requiring cross-reference to Article 51-52 classification procedures to determine applicability. This creates interpretive dependency on systemic risk classification.

where.text: as necessary

ambiguitySignal.reason: The obligation to cooperate 'as necessary' is conditional and indefinite. The scope of cooperation, the frequency, the form of cooperation, and the mechanisms are not specified. Determining when cooperation is 'necessary' requires case-by-case judgment.

It states compliance with harmonised standards grants 'presumption of conformity to the extent that those standards cover those obligations'—creating a partial presumption framework dependent on standard scope. Providers not adhering to an approved code or standard must demonstrate 'alternative adequate means for assessment by the Commission,' leaving the assessment criteria and expectations undefined.

The Commission's discretion in defining these methodologies is substantial but constrained by the legislative procedure under Article 97. The actual scope of methodologies is unknown until delegated acts are adopted.

referencesWithContext.context: The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with [Article 97 (2)](#article-97-2) to amend Annexes XI and XII in light of evolving technological developments.
referencesWithContext.snippet: delegated acts in accordance with Article 97 (2) to amend Annexes XI and XII

ambiguitySignal.reason: The provision empowers the Commission to amend Annexes XI and XII 'in light of evolving technological developments.' The threshold for amending the annexes, the scope of permissible amendments, and the process are not specified in Article 53 but deferred to Article 97. This creates open-ended discretion subject to legislative oversight mechanisms.

referencesWithContext.context: Any information or documentation obtained pursuant to this Article, including trade secrets, shall be treated in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in [Article 78](#article-78).
referencesWithContext.snippet: confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78

ambiguitySignal.reason: The provision imposes an obligation to treat information 'in accordance with confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78.' This creates a cross-reference dependency—the exact scope of confidentiality protection depends on Article 78's definition and procedures. The article delegates the specifics to another provision, introducing interpretive dependency.

chapters.heading: Article 54
chapters.title: Authorised representatives of providers of general-purpose AI models
chapters.fullTitle: Article 54: Authorised representatives of providers of general-purpose AI models

where.text: established in third countries

when.text: prior to placing a general-purpose AI model on the Union market

chapters.summaryText: Establishes the requirement for third-country providers of general-purpose AI models to appoint an authorised representative in the Union before market placement. Specifies the representative's tasks, mandate, and authority to be addressed on compliance matters.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The scope and responsibilities of the authorised representative are clearly specified, but the assessment of 'verification' compliance in paragraph 3(a) and what constitutes adequate 'cooperation' in paragraph 3(d) involve some interpretive judgment. The exception in paragraph 6 for free and open-source models with public parameters requires assessment of what constitutes 'systemic risks', which is delegated.

when.text: prior to placing a general-purpose AI model on the Union market

ambiguitySignal.reason: Direct obligation with clear timing, actor, and action. Third-country providers must designate a Union-established authorised representative via written mandate before market entry.
Obligation to enable the representative to perform mandate tasks. Clear and straightforward duty with no conditional or discretionary elements.

Paragraph 3 establishes core tasks, but the scope of 'verification' in point (a) and what constitutes adequate 'cooperation' in point (d) require judgment. The standard for demonstrating compliance in point (c) ('necessary to demonstrate compliance') is contextual.

referencesWithContext.context: verify that the technical documentation specified in Annex XI has been drawn up and all obligations referred to in Article 53 and, where applicable, Article 55 have been fulfilled by the provider;
referencesWithContext.snippet: technical documentation specified in Annex XI

referencesWithContext.context: verify that the technical documentation specified in Annex XI has been drawn up and all obligations referred to in Article 53 and, where applicable, Article 55 have been fulfilled by the provider;

referencesWithContext.snippet: where applicable, Article 55

where.text: applicable

ambiguitySignal.reason: The duty to 'verify' that obligations have been 'fulfilled' is clear in principle but requires judgment about the depth and scope of verification required. The conditional application ('where applicable') for Article 55 obligations depends on whether the model presents systemic risk, which is defined elsewhere.

referencesWithContext.context: keep a copy of the technical documentation specified in Annex XI at the disposal of the AI Office and national competent authorities, for a period of 10 years after the general-purpose AI model has been placed on the market
referencesWithContext.snippet: technical documentation specified in Annex XI

for.text: a period of 10 years after the general-purpose AI model has been placed on the market

ambiguitySignal.reason: Clear record-keeping obligation with specific duration (10 years), recipients (AI Office and national authorities), and content reference (Annex XI technical documentation and contact details). No ambiguous terms.

when.text: upon a reasoned request from the AI Office

ambiguitySignal.reason: The obligation to provide 'all information and documentation necessary to demonstrate compliance' is triggered by a 'reasoned request' from the AI Office. The interpretation of 'necessary' and what constitutes a 'reasoned request' involves some discretion.

when.text: upon a reasoned request from the AI Office and competent authorities

ambiguitySignal.reason: The duty to 'cooperate...in any action they take' is broad and contextual. The nature and extent of cooperation required is not specified and depends on the specific circumstances of enforcement actions. This allows for differing interpretations of the representative's obligations.
Clear procedural obligation for the mandate to empower the representative to be addressed by authorities on compliance matters. The phrase 'in addition to or instead of the provider' is unambiguous.

when.text: the representative considers or has reason to consider the provider acting contrary to obligations

ambiguitySignal.reason: The trigger for termination ('considers or has reason to consider the provider acting contrary to obligations') involves judgment about what factual basis is sufficient for the representative to believe there is a breach. The threshold of certainty required is not specified.

unless.text: the general-purpose AI models present systemic risks

ambiguitySignal.reason: The exception is framed as a savings clause ('shall not apply...unless'), but the assessment of whether a model 'presents systemic risks' is a factual determination that depends on definitions and criteria established in other articles. The scope of 'free and open-source' license and what constitutes 'publicly available' parameters requires reference to Article 3 definitions.

chapters.summaryText: Providers of general-purpose AI models determined to present systemic risk must perform model evaluation, assess and mitigate systemic risks, report incidents to the AI Office, and maintain cybersecurity protection. Compliance may be demonstrated through approved codes of practice or European harmonised standards.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The term 'systemic risk' is defined in Article 3 but its practical application to borderline GPAI systems involves judgment. 'Adequate level' of cybersecurity and 'appropriate' reporting procedures require interpretation. Implementation via codes of practice defers specifics.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Providers may rely on approved codes of practice (ready by 2 May 2025) or shall demonstrate alternative compliance means

referencesWithContext.snippet: in addition to the obligations listed in Articles 53 and 54

chapters.summaryText: Providers must perform model evaluation using state-of-the-art protocols, assess and mitigate systemic risks at Union level, report serious incidents to the AI Office, and ensure adequate cybersecurity protection.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Terms such as 'state of the art', 'systemic risks', 'appropriate' reporting, and 'adequate level' of cybersecurity are broadly stated without precise metrics. Implementation guidance is deferred to codes of practice and harmonised standards.
'State of the art' and 'appropriate' standardised protocols are not precisely defined. The scope and depth of adversarial testing required is subject to interpretation.
'Possible systemic risks' and 'appropriate' mitigation measures are not quantified. What constitutes adequate assessment and mitigation at Union level requires judgment and depends on risk profile.
The definition of 'serious incident' is not provided in the regulation. What constitutes 'relevant information' and the threshold for reporting 'without undue delay' involve practical judgment.
'Adequate level' of cybersecurity protection is not defined. Standards and technical specifications are not provided in the regulation itself.

Compliance with European harmonised standards grants presumption of conformity. Providers not adhering to a code or standard must demonstrate alternative adequate compliance means.

ambiguitySignal.reason: What constitutes 'adequate' alternative compliance means is not specified. The burden of proof and assessment standard by the Commission is not fully articulated.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Codes of practice ready by 2 May 2025; if not adequate by 2 August 2025, Commission may provide implementing acts

chapters.summaryText: Article 56 establishes a framework for codes of practice at Union level covering general-purpose AI models and high-risk systems. The AI Office and Board shall ensure codes cover obligations from Articles 53 and 55, with strict timelines for code readiness and Commission oversight through implementing acts.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Multiple undefined terms require interpretation: 'adequacy' of codes (para 6), 'appropriate' detail level for summaries (para 2-b), 'as appropriate' invitations (para 3), discretionary Commission intervention timeline (para 9). Scope of 'systemic risks at Union level' (para 2-c) versus individual systemic risks is ambiguous.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Codes must be ready by 2 May 2025. Fallback implementing acts available by 2 August 2025 if codes cannot be finalised or are deemed inadequate.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The term 'international approaches' is not further defined. Scope of 'facilitate' (must it be active coordination or passive support?) requires contextual interpretation.

where.text: appropriate

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 2(b) uses 'adequate level of detail' without defining adequacy. Paragraph 2(d) specifies 'proportionate' and 'taking into account' but these are evaluative standards. 'Appropriate' conditions in the heading sentence lacks specificity. Relationship between Union-level systemic risks (2-c) and individual model risks is not clearly delineated.

referencesWithContext.context: a id="article-56-2-a"> the means to ensure that the information referred to in [Article 53 (1)](#article-53-1), points (a) and (b), is kept up to date in light of market and technological developm
referencesWithContext.snippet: t the information referred to in Article 53 (1), points (a) and (b), is kept up to

ambiguitySignal.reason: Requirement is straightforward: codes must ensure information in Article 53(1) points (a)-(b) remains current considering market and technology changes. No ambiguity in scope or implementation.

'Adequate level of detail' is undefined. No objective criteria specified for determining what constitutes 'adequate' disclosure of training data summaries. Different stakeholders may have different interpretations of sufficiency.

'Systemic risks at Union level' is not precisely defined in relation to individual model risks. The phrase 'where appropriate' makes identification discretionary. The boundary between systemic and non-systemic risks requires contextual judgment per Article 51.

'Proportionate to the risks' requires balancing judgment but no criteria provided. 'Specific challenges' is vague. 'Ways in which such risks may emerge and materialise along the AI value chain' is open-ended and future-oriented, requiring predictive assessment.

'As appropriate' discretion on invitation scope and timing. Distinction between providers 'invited' to participate versus those who may 'support the process' is not clearly defined. No obligations specified on how to balance diverse stakeholder interests.

'As appropriate' KPIs are discretionary. 'Due account' of interests is subjective. No mechanism specified for resolving conflicting interests between different stakeholder groups (affected persons vs. industry efficiency). 'All interested parties' is potentially unbounded.

'Regularly' is not defined (e.g., quarterly, annually). 'As measured against' KPIs assumes KPIs exist and are measurable, but KPI specificity is deferred to codes themselves. Discretion on how to 'reflect differences in size and capacity' requires balancing guidance.

chapters.heading: Article 57
chapters.title: AI regulatory sandboxes
chapters.fullTitle: Article 57: AI regulatory sandboxes
chapters.summaryText: This article establishes the framework for AI regulatory sandboxes, controlled environments enabling innovation testing and development of AI systems with regulatory oversight before market deployment.

penaltyExposure.traceability: No direct penalties; sandbox activities have conditional immunity under article-57-12

ambiguitySignal.reason: Terms like 'appropriate', 'relevant', and 'sufficient resources' require discretionary judgment by member states; confidentiality provisions in article-57-8 create interpretive scope for what may be publicly disclosed.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113 general application (except article-57-1 which implies 2026-08-02 operational deadline)
temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Member States must ensure operational sandbox by 2 August 2026; pre-existing sandboxes may be leveraged.

when.text: by 2 August 2026

chapters.summaryText: Member States must establish at least one operational AI regulatory sandbox nationally by 2 August 2026, with option for joint establishment and Commission support.

penaltyExposure.traceability: No direct penalty provision; enforcement via supervisory authority

ambiguitySignal.reason: Obligation is explicitly stated with precise deadline; flexibility in joint arrangements and Commission support is permissive, not mandatory.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113, with specific operational deadline of 2 August 2026 stated in this paragraph

chapters.summaryText: Member States may establish additional AI regulatory sandboxes at regional or local level, or jointly with other Member States.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Simple permissive statement with no mandatory conditions.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Permissive provision allowing EDPS discretion.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Terms 'sufficient resources' and 'appropriate' lack precise quantitative thresholds; assessment depends on member state circumstances and sandbox scope.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Terms 'controlled environment', 'fosters innovation', and 'facilitates development' are discretionary; implementation depends on member state judgment and sandbox design.

where.text: appropriate

chapters.summaryText: Competent authorities must provide guidance, supervision and support within sandboxes to identify and mitigate risks to fundamental rights, health, safety, and regulatory compliance.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Nature and intensity of guidance, supervision and support depend on contextual assessment of risks and member state resources; 'as appropriate' permits discretion.

chapters.summaryText: Competent authorities must provide guidance on regulatory expectations; issue written proof of successfully completed activities; and provide exit reports with learning outcomes for use in conformity assessment.

ambiguitySignal.reason: What constitutes 'learning outcomes' and 'reasonable extent' for accelerating conformity assessment is discretionary; market surveillance authorities and notified bodies may apply different weightings.

where.text: relevant

chapters.summaryText: AI regulatory sandboxes must be designed to achieve five key objectives: improving legal certainty, supporting best practice sharing, fostering innovation and competitiveness, contributing to evidence-based regulatory learning, and facilitating market access particularly for SMEs.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Aspirational framework; no direct penalties for non-achievement

ambiguitySignal.reason: Objectives are aspirational rather than prescriptive; terms like 'improving legal certainty', 'fostering innovation', and 'facilitating access' lack measurable criteria or thresholds.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Straightforward statement of objective without ambiguous conditions.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Simple objective statement.

chapters.summaryText: Sandboxes contribute to evidence-based regulatory learning.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Straightforward objective.

chapters.summaryText: Sandboxes facilitate and accelerate access to the Union market for AI systems, particularly for SMEs and start-ups.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Objective statement.

when.text: implementing legal provisions in respect of a specific AI regulatory sandbox project
