penaltyExposure.traceability: Real-world testing governance requirement; no direct penalty exposure

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Appropriate safeguards' is qualitative. Different national competent authorities may assess appropriateness differently based on national context and risk profiles.

chapters.heading: Article 59
chapters.title: Further processing of personal data for developing certain AI systems in the public interest in the AI regulatory sandbox
chapters.fullTitle: Article 59: Further processing of personal data for developing certain AI systems in the public interest in the AI regulatory sandbox

where.text: in the AI regulatory sandbox
all of the following conditions are met

when.text: AI systems shall be developed for safeguarding substantial public interest

unless.text: provided otherwise by Union or national law

chapters.summaryText: Permits further processing of personal data in AI regulatory sandboxes for developing AI systems serving substantial public interest when cumulative conditions are met. Extends to law enforcement under same conditions. Confirms paragraph 1 does not prejudice Union or national law on data processing and innovation.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 provider obligations (data protection, documentation, technical requirements)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 59-1 permits further processing subject to ten cumulative conditions. 'Substantial public interest' (59-1-a) is normatively open; specific areas are enumerated but extensibility unclear. 'Effective monitoring mechanisms' (59-1-c) lacks specification. 'Appropriately protected' (59-1-g) delegates to operator judgment. Paragraph 3 creates a savings clause (without prejudice) that preserves Union/national law exceptions to processing restrictions, making full scope contingent on external legal frameworks.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113 general application (Chapter VI not listed in exceptions)

where.text: in the AI regulatory sandbox

when.text: all of the following conditions are met

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 (provider obligations on documentation, technical measures, data protection)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 1 specifies a gateway (all conditions must be met) but several conditions use discretionary language: 'effective monitoring mechanisms' (59-1-c, no specification of what constitutes effectiveness); 'appropriately protected' (59-1-g, operator discretion on technical/organisational measures); 'necessary' and 'effective' (59-1-b, contextual assessment required). The condition structure is permissive (personal data lawfully collected 'may be processed') but cumulative, creating interaction ambiguity across ten sub-conditions.

when.text: AI systems shall be developed for safeguarding substantial public interest
in one or more of the following areas

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 (scope condition for lawful processing)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (a) requires 'substantial public interest'. 'Substantial' is normatively open. Seven sub-areas are enumerated (i-vii) but the relationship between 'substantial' and area-listing is unclear: is presence in one area sufficient, or must there be substantiality within that area? 'Improvement of health care systems' and 'energy sustainability' are broad descriptors. Determination of 'public interest' may be subject to operator, authority, or national law interpretation.

referencesWithContext.context: necessary for complying with one or more of the requirements referred to in Chapter III Section 2 where those requirements cannot effectively be fulfilled by processing anonymised, synthetic or other non-personal data
referencesWithContext.snippet: requirements referred to in Chapter III Section 2 where those requirements cannot effectively be fulfilled

where.text: necessary for complying with requirements in Chapter III Section 2

when.text: those requirements cannot effectively be fulfilled by processing anonymised, synthetic or other non-personal data

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (b) requires that 'those requirements cannot effectively be fulfilled' by non-personal data alternatives. 'Effectively' is evaluative and context-dependent. No guidance on who determines effectiveness (provider, authority, or technical review). Reference to 'Chapter III Section 2' (Articles 16-25) creates broad applicability, and 'one or more of the requirements' allows partial applicability. The threshold for necessity versus mere convenience is not specified.

where.text: during sandbox experimentation

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (c) requires 'effective monitoring mechanisms' to identify 'high risks' as per GDPR Article 35 and EUDPR Article 39 (Data Protection Impact Assessments). It also requires 'response mechanisms to promptly mitigate those risks' and ability to 'stop the processing'. The content, specificity, and scope of monitoring/response are not defined in the AI Act, delegated to GDPR/EUDPR frameworks and operator implementation. 'Promptly' and 'appropriately' are context-dependent.

where.text: any personal data to be processed in the context of the sandbox

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (d) requires a 'functionally separate, isolated and protected data processing environment'. The terms 'functionally separate', 'isolated', and 'protected' are not defined. 'Under the control of the prospective provider' is clear, but 'only authorised persons have access' leaves discretion to the provider on who qualifies as 'authorised'. No specification of technical standards or certification required.

where.text: any personal data created in the sandbox

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (e) establishes two clear rules: (1) originally collected data can be shared only under Union data protection law (GDPR/EUDPR); (2) sandbox-created data cannot be shared outside the sandbox. The distinction between 'originally collected' and 'sandbox-created' is operative, though distinguishing them may require case-by-case assessment.

where.text: any processing of personal data in the context of the sandbox

scope: no measures or decisions affecting data subjects
no impact on Union data protection rights

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 (data subject protection and use restrictions)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (f) prohibits processing that 'leads to measures or decisions affecting the data subjects'. The scope of 'affects' is broad: does it include indirect effects, reputational impacts, or only direct legal/administrative decisions? The phrase 'does not affect the application of their rights' is clear on rights preservation but interaction with real-world harms from sandbox AI testing remains contextual.

where.text: any personal data processed in the context of the sandbox

when.text: once the participation in the sandbox has terminated or the personal data has reached the end of its retention period

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 (technical and organizational measures, data deletion)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (g) requires 'appropriate technical and organisational measures' (undefined as to specificity, standards, or certification) and data deletion 'once the participation in the sandbox has terminated'. The timing and scope of deletion (complete erasure, anonymization, archival) is not specified. 'Appropriate' delegates to provider and context-specific assessment.

where.text: the logs of the processing of personal data in the context of the sandbox

when.text: for the duration of the participation in the sandbox

unless.text: provided otherwise by Union or national law

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (h) requires logs 'for the duration of the participation in the sandbox'. What constitutes 'logs'—API calls, data access events, transformation operations, or all of the above—is not specified. The savings clause 'unless provided otherwise by Union or national law' creates potential variability across member states and by legal basis (GDPR Article 33, confidentiality laws, etc.).

referencesWithContext.context: a complete and detailed description of the process and rationale behind the training, testing and validation of the AI system is kept together with the testing results as part of the technical documentation referred to in Annex IV
referencesWithContext.snippet: technical documentation referred to in Annex IV

where.text: a complete and detailed description of the process and rationale behind the training, testing and validation of the AI system

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 (technical documentation requirements per Annex IV)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (i) requires 'a complete and detailed description' kept in technical documentation per Annex IV. The standard of 'completeness' and 'detail' is not specified in Article 59 but deferred to Annex IV (high-risk AI system requirements). The interaction between sandbox-specific documentation and standard Annex IV requirements is left to provider interpretation and competent authority review.

where.text: a short summary of the AI project developed in the sandbox, its objectives and expected results

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-50 transparency obligations (or article-16 provider transparency)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (j) requires publication of 'a short summary' on competent authorities' websites. 'Short' is relative and not quantified. The exception for 'sensitive operational data' in law enforcement, border control, immigration, and asylum contexts is normatively open: what qualifies as 'sensitive' depends on context, threat assessment, and national law. The provider/authority boundary for determining sensitivity is not specified.

referencesWithContext.context: For the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including safeguarding against and preventing threats to public security, under the control and responsibility of law enforcement authorities, the processing of personal data in AI regulatory sandboxes shall be based on a specific Union or national law and subject to the same cumulative conditions as referred to in paragraph 1
referencesWithContext.snippet: subject to the same cumulative conditions as referred to in paragraph 1

when.text: for the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties
including safeguarding against and preventing threats to public security

where.text: under the control and responsibility of law enforcement authorities
based on a specific Union or national law
subject to the same cumulative conditions as referred to in paragraph 1

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-16 or article-26 (deployer/law enforcement obligations)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 2 extends Article 59-1 conditions to law enforcement but requires compliance with 'a specific Union or national law'. The legal basis, scope, and proportionality of law enforcement sandbox processing are delegated to member state and Union law (GDPR Articles 6(3)-(4), national security exemptions, police directive). The interaction between GDPR/EUDPR and AI Act sandbox rules for law enforcement remains legally complex.

referencesWithContext.context: Paragraph 1 is without prejudice to Union or national law which excludes processing of personal data for other purposes than those explicitly mentioned in that law, as well as to Union or national law laying down the basis for the processing of personal data which is necessary for the purpose of developing, testing or training of innovative AI systems or any other legal basis, in compliance with Union law on the protection of personal data.
referencesWithContext.snippet: Paragraph 1 is without prejudice to Union or national law

without_prejudice_to.text: Union or national law which excludes processing of personal data for other purposes than those explicitly mentioned in that law
Union or national law laying down the basis for the processing of personal data which is necessary for the purpose of developing, testing or training of innovative AI systems or any other legal basis

where.text: in compliance with Union law on the protection of personal data

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 3 is a savings clause preserving Union and national law exceptions to the Article 59-1 regime. It creates a broad carve-out: member states and Union law can exclude processing, establish alternative bases, or provide other legal grounds for innovative AI sandbox processing. The scope of 'any other legal basis, in compliance with Union law on the protection of personal data' is open-ended and contingent on external legal frameworks. This creates legal uncertainty about when Article 59-1 applies versus when national or alternative frameworks govern.

chapters.heading: Article 60
chapters.title: Testing of high-risk AI systems in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes
chapters.fullTitle: Article 60: Testing of high-risk AI systems in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes
chapters.summaryText: Article 60 establishes a framework for testing high-risk AI systems in real-world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes. Providers or prospective providers may conduct such testing subject to approval by market surveillance authorities and strict conditions protecting data, vulnerable groups, informed consent, and system reversibility.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The framework imposes clear obligations and prohibitions, but several terms require interpretation: 'real world conditions', 'appropriate' protections for vulnerable groups (art. 60-4-g), timing of serious incident reporting, and scope of 'immediate mitigation measures' (art. 60-7) depend on context and implementing guidance.

referencesWithContext.context: ay be conducted by providers or prospective providers of high-risk AI systems listed in [Annex III](#annex-iii), in accordance with this Article and the real-world testing plan referred to in this Art
referencesWithContext.snippet: ers of high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III, in accordance with this Article and t

referencesWithContext.context: d testing plan referred to in this Article, without prejudice to the prohibitions under [Article 5](#article-5).
referencesWithContext.snippet: t prejudice to the prohibitions under Article 5.

referencesWithContext.context: 1. Testing of high-risk AI systems in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes may be conducted by providers or prospective providers of high-risk AI systems lis
referencesWithContext.snippet: 1. Testing of high-risk AI systems in real world conditions outside AI reg

referencesWithContext.snippet: ers of high-risk AI systems listed in Annex III, in accordance with this Article and t

without_prejudice_to.text: the prohibitions under [Article 5](#article-5)
Union or national law on the testing in real world conditions of high-risk AI systems related to products covered by Union harmonisation legislation listed in [Annex I](#annex-i)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 1 clearly establishes provider/prospective provider permission to test high-risk systems subject to real-world testing plan compliance and Article 5 restrictions. Implementing acts for detailed elements are delegated to Commission via Article 98(2), so specifics are deferred but permission is unambiguous.

referencesWithContext.context: ers or prospective providers may conduct testing of high-risk AI systems referred to in [Annex III](#annex-iii) in real world conditions at any time before the placing on the market or the putting int
referencesWithContext.snippet: f high-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III in real world conditions at any time b

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 2 grants explicit permission ('may conduct') for testing at any lifecycle stage before deployment. Permission scope is clear; 'real world conditions' is discussed further in substantive conditions of para 4.

without_prejudice_to.text: any ethical review that is required by Union or national law

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 3 is a savings clause clarifying that Article 60 testing does not supersede ethical review requirements in Union or national law. Its relationship to existing obligations is explicit ('without prejudice to').

where.text: all of the following conditions are met

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 4 sets out 11 conditions (a-k) with several vague or context-dependent terms: 'appropriate' protection for vulnerable groups (g), 'immediately' adopted mitigation measures, 'real world conditions' scope, and the meaning of 'overseen' and 'suitably qualified' (j). The 30-day tacit approval mechanism (b) also presents implementation uncertainty across Member States.

Point (a) mandates submission of a real-world testing plan to the competent market surveillance authority in the relevant Member State. The obligation is unambiguous; scope of what constitutes an adequate plan may be clarified by Commission implementing acts (para 1).

where.text: the market surveillance authority has not provided an answer within 30 days
national law does not provide for a tacit approval

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (b) creates a 30-day approval mechanism with tacit approval provisions dependent on national law implementation. The interplay between Union-level timeline and variable Member State law creates interpretive uncertainty: does 'tacit approval' apply uniformly, or only where national law explicitly provides? How do Member States handle mixed authorization regimes?

referencesWithContext.context: e exception of providers or prospective providers of high-risk AI systems referred to in points [1](#annex-iii-1), [6](#annex-iii-6) and [7](#annex-iii-7) of [Annex III](#annex-iii) in the areas of la

referencesWithContext.snippet: of high-risk AI systems referred to in points 1, 6 and

referencesWithContext.snippet: and high-risk AI systems referred to in point 2 of Annex III has regis

referencesWithContext.context: #annex-iii) has registered the testing in real world conditions in accordance with [Article 71 (4)](#article-71-4) with a Union-wide unique single identification number and with the information specif
referencesWithContext.snippet: ld conditions in accordance with Article 71 (4) with a Union-wide unique single ide

referencesWithContext.context: h a Union-wide unique single identification number and with the information specified in [Annex IX](#annex-ix); the provider or prospective provider of high-risk AI systems referred to in points [1](#
referencesWithContext.snippet: and with the information specified in Annex IX; the provider or prospective provider o

referencesWithContext.context: ditions in the secure non-public section of the EU database according to [Article 49(4), point (d)](#article-49-4-d), with a Union-wide unique single identification number and with the information spe
referencesWithContext.snippet: database according to Article 49(4), point (d), with a Union-wide unique single

referencesWithContext.context: #annex-iii) has registered the testing in real-world conditions in accordance with [Article 49 (5)](#article-49-5);
referencesWithContext.snippet: ld conditions in accordance with Article 49 (5);

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (c) creates different registration pathways based on AI system type and use case (law enforcement, migration vs. others; Annex III points 1, 6, 7 vs. point 2). The detailed requirements for Union-wide identification and information submission are specified in Annex IX and Articles 49, 71 – specific scope and implementation details are deferred to those normative references.

Point (d) mandates that testing providers/prospective providers either be Union-established or have appointed a Union-based legal representative. The requirement is unambiguous; scope of 'established' may depend on Union entity law interpretations, but the obligation is clear.

Point (e) restricts third-country data transfers to cases with 'appropriate and applicable safeguards under Union law'. The terms 'appropriate' and 'applicable' are context-dependent and may be clarified by reference to GDPR Chapter V adequacy decisions and standard contractual clauses, but the scope of required safeguards is not self-executing.

Point (f) sets a 6-month testing cap (extendable by another 6 months with notification and justification). Ambiguity arises from the phrase 'not longer than necessary to achieve its objectives' – what constitutes 'necessary' is contextual and subject to interpretation by testing providers and market surveillance authorities.

Point (g) requires 'appropriate' protection for subjects in vulnerable groups (age or disability). The term 'appropriate' is vague and context-dependent; there is no threshold or specification of what measures constitute adequate protection. Implementation will depend on ethics committees and market surveillance authority judgment.

referencesWithContext.context: rticipate, and given the relevant instructions for use of the AI system referred to in [Article 13](#article-13); the provider or prospective provider and the deployer or prospective deployer shall co
referencesWithContext.snippet: use of the AI system referred to in Article 13; the provider or prospective provider

where.text: a provider or prospective provider organises the testing in real world conditions in cooperation with one or more deployers or prospective deployers

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (h) requires providers to inform deployers of 'relevant to their decision to participate' aspects and to conclude agreements specifying 'roles and responsibilities'. The scope of 'relevant' and the granularity of 'roles and responsibilities' are context-dependent and may vary based on the testing scenario and deployer expertise.

referencesWithContext.context: of the testing in real world conditions have given informed consent in accordance with [Article 61](#article-61), or in the case of law enforcement, where the seeking of informed consent would prevent
referencesWithContext.snippet: informed consent in accordance with Article 61, or in the case of law enforcement, w

where.text: subjects of testing have given informed consent in accordance with Article 61
law enforcement exception: seeking informed consent would prevent AI system testing

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (i) provides a law enforcement exception to informed consent where seeking it would prevent testing. The phrase 'would prevent' is interpretive: what threshold of interference constitutes 'prevention'? For non-law-enforcement testing, Article 61 informed consent is mandatory; scope of what constitutes adequate consent is defined there but depends on implementation.

Point (j) requires testing to be 'effectively overseen' by 'suitably qualified' persons with 'necessary capacity, training and authority'. Terms like 'effectively', 'suitably', and 'necessary' are vague and context-dependent. There is no specification of minimum qualification standards, training curricula, or authority thresholds; these will depend on system type and testing scope.

Point (k) mandates that AI system predictions, recommendations, or decisions 'can be effectively reversed and disregarded'. The requirement is clear: systems must allow human override of outputs. 'Effectively' implies practical, not merely technical, reversibility, but the core obligation is unambiguous.

Paragraph 5 establishes testing subject rights to withdraw consent without detriment and request data deletion. The rights are clearly stated; their enforcement depends on provider and member state implementation of data protection and subject safeguards.

referencesWithContext.context: 6. In accordance with [Article 75](#article-75), Member States shall confer on their market surveillance authorities the powers of requiring providers and prospective provide
referencesWithContext.snippet: rticle-60-6"> In accordance with Article 75, Member States shall confer on their

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 6 mandates Member States to confer surveillance powers (information requests, unannounced inspections) per Article 75. The obligation is clear; specific scope and implementation of surveillance powers depend on Article 75 and national enforcement procedures.

referencesWithContext.context: ons shall be reported to the national market surveillance authority in accordance with [Article 73](#article-73). The provider or prospective provider shall adopt immediate mitigation measures or, fai
referencesWithContext.snippet: illance authority in accordance with Article 73. The provider or prospective provider

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 7 requires reporting of 'serious incidents' and 'immediate' mitigation measures. The term 'serious incident' depends on Article 73 definition; 'immediate' is temporally vague (what timeframe is immediate?). Scope of mitigation (suspension vs. termination decision) depends on incident assessment and context.

Paragraph 8 mandates notification to market surveillance authorities of testing suspension/termination and final outcomes. The obligation is clear; scope and format of final outcomes reporting may be specified in implementing guidance or Article 98 acts.

Paragraph 9 establishes provider liability under applicable Union and national liability law for damages caused by testing. The obligation is clear; scope of liability is determined by applicable liability regimes (product liability, civil law, etc.), not by this Regulation.

chapters.heading: Article 61
chapters.title: Informed consent to participate in testing in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes
chapters.fullTitle: Article 61: Informed consent to participate in testing in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes

applicability.scopeNote: applies outside regulatory sandboxes (covered by Article 60)

chapters.summaryText: Providers must obtain freely-given informed consent from test subjects before conducting real-world testing outside regulatory sandboxes. Consent must be documented and cover testing nature, conditions, participant rights, and system decision reversal mechanisms.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The term 'duly informed' and requirements for 'concise, clear, relevant, and understandable information' are subjective. The point (d) reference to 'arrangements for requesting reversal or disregarding' predictions lacks specificity on scope and timing of such requests.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113 (general application, Chapter VI not listed for earlier entry)

referencesWithContext.context: For the purpose of testing in real world conditions under [Article 60](#article-60), freely-given informed consent shall be obtained from the subjects of testing prior to
referencesWithContext.snippet: sting in real world conditions under Article 60, freely-given informed consent shall

when.text: testing in real world conditions outside AI regulatory sandboxes

ambiguitySignal.reason: The requirement that subjects be 'duly informed with concise, clear, relevant, and understandable information' is subjective. The plural form 'subjects' suggests group testing but the mechanics of joint consent vs. individual consent is not specified.

where.text: in the consent disclosure

ambiguitySignal.reason: The phrase 'possible inconvenience that may be linked to their participation' is indefinite. The scope of 'inconvenience' (physical, psychological, time burden, etc.) is not defined, leaving implementation discretion.
Obligation to disclose testing conditions and duration is straightforward and testable.
Rights to refuse and withdraw are clearly stated with explicit protection against detriment. This is aligned with fundamental data subject rights and consent principles.

The requirement to disclose 'arrangements for requesting reversal or disregarding' is highly indefinite. The scope of which system outputs can be reversed, the timing and process for requests, and success criteria are not specified. This depends on the specific AI system design.

referencesWithContext.context: the Union-wide unique single identification number of the testing in real world conditions in accordance with [Article 60 (4)](#article-60-4) point \(c\), and the contact details of the provider or its legal representative from
referencesWithContext.snippet: ld conditions in accordance with Article 60 (4) point \(c\), and the contact detail

ambiguitySignal.reason: Obligation to provide identification number (assigned per Article 60(4)(c)) and contact details is clear and verifiable.

where.text: documenting consent

ambiguitySignal.reason: The requirement that consent be 'dated and documented' does not specify the required format (electronic, physical, etc.), the minimum level of detail, retention periods, or what constitutes adequate proof of consent.

chapters.heading: Article 62
chapters.title: Measures for providers and deployers, in particular SMEs, including start-ups
chapters.fullTitle: Article 62: Measures for providers and deployers, in particular SMEs, including start-ups

referencesWithContext.context: fees for conformity assessment under [Article 43](#article-43), reducing those fees proportionately
referencesWithContext.snippet: fees for conformity assessment under Article 43

where.text: in relation to supporting innovation in SMEs and start-ups

chapters.summaryText: Member States and the AI Office shall undertake specific measures to support AI innovation, including priority access to regulatory sandboxes for SMEs, training activities, communication channels, and efforts to maintain affordable conformity assessment fees and provide standardised templates and information platforms.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The article establishes obligations but with flexible implementation criteria (e.g., 'organise specific awareness raising', 'where appropriate', 'as appropriate'). The scope and adequacy of these measures depend on regulatory interpretation and MS discretion.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113 (general application, Chapter VI not carved out)

where.text: in relation to SMEs and start-ups with registered office or branch in Union

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 1 establishes MS obligations using 'shall undertake the following actions' with sub-points specifying areas for action. However, the precise scope of 'awareness raising', 'specific' training, and adequacy of 'communication channels' requires interpretation.

where.text: SMEs and start-ups with registered office or branch in Union

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (a) requires 'priority access' to sandboxes but subject to 'eligibility conditions and selection criteria'. The definition of priority in relation to other operators, and the scope of eligibility, remain subject to MS discretion and delegated acts.

where.text: tailored to the needs of SMEs, start-ups, deployers, and where appropriate local public authorities

ambiguitySignal.reason: The phrase 'specific awareness raising and training activities' is open-ended. The regulation does not specify: frequency, duration, format, accessibility standards, minimum content, or success criteria. The phrase 'as appropriate' for local public authorities further expands discretion.

where.text: for communication with SMEs, start-ups, deployers, innovators and local public authorities

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (c) requires MS to 'utilise existing dedicated channels' and 'where appropriate, establish new ones'. The standard for determining what is 'appropriate' and the expected responsiveness/coverage of channels are not specified.

where.text: in standardisation development process

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (d) requires MS to 'facilitate the participation' without specifying: the scope of the standardisation process, what constitutes effective facilitation, how access barriers should be removed, or how MS involvement relates to CEN/CENELEC/ETSI activities.

referencesWithContext.context: fees for conformity assessment under [Article 43](#article-43), reducing those fees proportionately to their size, market size and other relevant indicators
referencesWithContext.snippet: fees for conformity assessment under Article 43

when.text: setting the fees for conformity assessment

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 2 requires fees to be 'taken into account' and reduced 'proportionately' but does not specify: the calculation methodology, what constitutes proportionate reduction, how 'market size' and 'other relevant indicators' should be weighted, or enforcement mechanisms.

Paragraph 3 establishes AI Office obligations with general language ('undertake the following actions') but implementation details depend on Board requests (templates), discretionary design choices (information platform), and MS cooperation (best practices convergence).

when.text: as specified by the Board in its request

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (a) makes template provision contingent on Board requests. The scope, content, and updates of templates depend on Board determinations, not the regulation itself. This is a delegated implementation mechanism.

Point (b) requires development and maintenance of a 'single information platform providing easy to use information'. The standards for 'easy to use', platform functionality, content coverage, accessibility requirements, and maintenance frequency are not specified.

Point (c) requires the AI Office to 'organise appropriate communication campaigns'. The standards for 'appropriate' scope, frequency, target audiences, channels, and success metrics are not defined in the regulation.

Point (d) requires AI Office to 'evaluate and promote the convergence of best practices'. The scope of 'best practices', the mechanism and success criteria for promoting convergence, and the relationship to existing procurement frameworks are not specified.

chapters.heading: Article 63
chapters.title: Derogations for specific operators
chapters.fullTitle: Article 63: Derogations for specific operators

conditions.when: Microenterprises may comply with simplified QMS elements if they do not have partner or linked enterprises

chapters.summaryText: Permits microenterprises without partner or linked enterprises to comply with simplified quality management system elements. Directs Commission to develop guidelines on simplification without reducing protection levels or high-risk AI system compliance.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article permits simplified compliance but does not specify which QMS elements may be simplified; delegates specificity to Commission guidelines. Scope of 'partner enterprises' and 'linked enterprises' depends on Recommendation 2003/361/EC definition, which is external.

referencesWithContext.context: uality management system required by Article 17 of this Regulation in a simplified manner
referencesWithContext.snippet: Microenterprises... may comply with certain elements of the quality management system required by Article 17 of this Regulation in a simplified manner

conditions.when: Microenterprises without partner or linked enterprises

ambiguitySignal.reason: Permits simplified QMS compliance but Commission guidelines determine which specific elements qualify. Scope of permitted simplification deferred to implementing guidance. Clarity depends on external Recommendation definition of 'partner' and 'linked' enterprises.

referencesWithContext.context: Paragraph 1 of this Article shall not be interpreted as exempting those operators from fulfilling any other requirements or obligations laid down in this Regulation
referencesWithContext.snippet: Paragraph 1 of this Article shall not be interpreted as exempting those operators from fulfilling any other requirements or obligations

conditions.when: Interpretation of Article 63(1)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Explicit savings clause preventing misinterpretation of Article 63(1) as a broad exemption. Unambiguously confirms microenterprises remain bound by all other Regulation requirements, with specific enumeration (Articles 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 72, 73).

chapters.heading: Article 64
chapters.title: AI Office
chapters.fullTitle: Article 64: AI Office
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the AI Office under the Commission to develop Union expertise and capabilities in artificial intelligence. Member States must facilitate the tasks entrusted to the AI Office as specified throughout the regulation.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article establishes institutional framework with clear obligations. The Commission shall develop expertise through the AI Office; Member States shall facilitate its tasks. No conditional language or discretionary terms requiring interpretation.
Direct obligation: The Commission shall develop Union expertise and capabilities in AI through the AI Office. Clear subject, modal verb, and mechanism.

Obligation requires Member States to 'facilitate' tasks. The scope of 'facilitate' is contextual and depends on implementation guidance from Articles 65-67 and other parts of the regulation that specify AI Office tasks.

chapters.heading: Article 65
chapters.title: Establishment and structure of the European Artificial Intelligence Board
chapters.fullTitle: Article 65: Establishment and structure of the European Artificial Intelligence Board
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the European Artificial Intelligence Board (Board) composed of Member State representatives, observers, and invited authorities. Defines Board composition, member designation, competences, representation requirements, rules of procedure adoption, and standing sub-groups for market surveillance and notified bodies oversight. Sets Board governance principles including neutrality, secretariat functions by the AI Office, and operational procedures.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article defines governance structure and procedural requirements. Primary ambiguity: scope of 'relevant competences' (paragraph 4-a) and 'consistency and coordination' mechanisms (paragraph 4-c) depend on national legal frameworks and interpretation of regulatory requirements elsewhere in the regulation. Term 'as appropriate' for standing sub-groups (paragraph 6) leaves discretion over formation of additional sub-groups.
Declaratory provision establishing the Board as an EU body. No ambiguity in intent or scope.

where.text: the issues discussed are of relevance for them

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph sets composition and observer roles. Ambiguity: whether 'participation as observer' by EDPS and 'attendance without voting' by AI Office creates obligations on these bodies to participate, or merely permits it. Discretion on inviting 'other national and Union authorities' based on case-by-case relevance determination lacks objective criteria.
Specifies the mandate period and renewal conditions for Member State representatives. Fixed term (3 years, renewable once) provides clarity on turnover frequency.

Paragraph requires representatives to have 'relevant competences and powers' and ensure 'consistency and coordination' without explicit criteria. Sub-point (b) on 'single contact point' requires discretionary judgment about 'where appropriate' for stakeholder engagement. Sub-point (c) delegation to facilitate consistency depends on existing national authorities' structure and AI Act implementation progress.

referencesWithContext.context: have the relevant competences and powers in their Member State so as to contribute actively to the achievement of the Board's tasks referred to in Article 66
referencesWithContext.snippet: tasks referred to in Article 66

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Relevant competences and powers' is not objectively defined—depends on Member State legal structures and interpretation of Board tasks in Article 66. What constitutes 'relevant' to enable 'active contribution' is context-dependent and subject to national discretion.

where.text: where appropriate, taking into account Member States' needs

ambiguitySignal.reason: Point (b) contains discretionary language: 'where appropriate, taking into account Member States' needs' is inherently subjective. No objective criteria define what constitutes appropriate or which needs require a dedicated stakeholder contact point vs. general representation.

Point (c) uses 'are empowered to facilitate consistency and coordination' without specifying what mechanisms achieve this. Terms 'consistency,' 'coordination,' and 'relevant data and information' lack objective definition, depending on interpretation of Board's regulatory role and national AI Act implementation frameworks.

Paragraph specifies that Member State representatives adopt rules of procedure by two-thirds majority and details the matters to be covered (selection, mandate, voting, sub-group organization). Clear procedural requirements, though detailed rules themselves are delegated to Member States.

referencesWithContext.context: The Board may establish other standing or temporary sub-groups as appropriate for the purpose of examining specific issues. Where appropriate, representatives of the advisory forum referred to in Article 67 may be invited to such sub-groups
referencesWithContext.snippet: advisory forum referred to in Article 67 may be invited

where.text: appropriate

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph requires establishment of two standing sub-groups but uses 'as appropriate' to determine when other temporary sub-groups are formed. No clear criteria define what issues warrant temporary sub-group creation. Reference to ADCO (Regulation 2019/1020) adds interpretive layer on administrative cooperation scope.

Requires Board to be 'organised and operated so as to safeguard the objectivity and impartiality of its activities' without specifying what organizational measures constitute adequate safeguards. Effectiveness depends on Board's internal rules and governance culture.

Specifies Board Chair role (Member State representative), AI Office secretariat duties (convening meetings, agenda preparation), and procedural requirements. Clear assignment of operational responsibilities.

chapters.heading: Article 66
chapters.title: Tasks of the Board
chapters.fullTitle: Article 66: Tasks of the Board
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the European Artificial Intelligence Board's duties to advise the Commission and Member States, coordinate national authorities, develop best practices, issue recommendations on implementation matters, and support consistent enforcement of the Regulation including rules on general-purpose AI models.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The article provides a comprehensive enumeration of Board tasks with clear scope statements ('shall advise and assist', 'may in particular'). Each sub-task specifies the nature of the Board's role.

conditions.when: To facilitate the consistent and effective application of this Regulation

ambiguitySignal.reason: The paragraph establishes the Board's core mandate ('shall advise and assist') with a clear purpose statement and introduces an enumerated list of specific tasks via 'may in particular'.

referencesWithContext.context: support joint activities of market surveillance authorities referred to in Article 74 (11)
referencesWithContext.snippet: ance authorities referred to in Article 74 (11);

ambiguitySignal.reason: The Board 'may' contribute to coordination and support joint activities, but the scope of 'contribute' and conditions for 'cooperation with and subject to the agreement of' market surveillance authorities leave implementation details to practice.
Task is clearly stated: collect and share expertise and best practices. The mechanism and frequency are left to Board discretion, which is appropriate for a governance coordination role.
The Board's advisory role on implementation is clearly defined, with particular emphasis on general-purpose AI model enforcement. The advice is non-binding guidance.

referencesWithContext.context: in relation to the derogation from the conformity assessment procedures referred to in Article 46
referencesWithContext.snippet: assessment procedures referred to in Article 46, the functioning of AI regulatory san

ambiguitySignal.reason: The harmonisation task is clearly scoped to specific topics (conformity assessment derogation, regulatory sandboxes, real-world testing), with explicit Article references providing definitional anchors.

referencesWithContext.snippet: - (e) at the request of the Commission or on its own initiative, issu

ambiguitySignal.reason: The Board's recommendation and opinion authority is clearly triggered ('at the request of the Commission or on its own initiative') with an extensive but well-defined enumeration of topics ranging from codes of conduct to potential regulatory amendments.

The Board's role in 'promoting AI literacy, public awareness and understanding' is broadly stated without specifying mechanisms, target audiences, or success criteria. Implementation depends on Commission-Board coordination.

The task to 'facilitate the development of common criteria and a shared understanding' is stated, but the degree of formality, binding nature, and process for developing such criteria and benchmarks are not specified.

The Board's cooperation mandate with other EU institutions and expert groups is clearly stated with specific policy domains enumerated. The 'as appropriate' qualifier grants necessary flexibility for effective governance coordination.
The Board's role in contributing to international cooperation is clearly stated. The scope appropriately remains flexible to accommodate evolving international AI governance arrangements.

The Board's assistance role in developing expertise and assessing training needs is clearly defined. The collaborative nature ('assist') appropriately recognizes that competent authorities retain primary responsibility.
The Board's support role for the AI Office regarding regulatory sandboxes and facilitation of inter-sandbox cooperation and information-sharing is clearly stated.
The Board's contribution to guidance document development is clearly stated. The dual role ('contribute to, and provide relevant advice on') encompasses both substantive input and advisory review.
The Board's advisory role to the Commission on international AI matters is clearly stated. The broad scope is appropriate given the dynamic nature of international AI policy.

The Board's role in providing opinions to the Commission on qualified alerts regarding general-purpose AI models is clearly defined. This complements the regulatory framework for systemic risk monitoring.

The Board's role in receiving Member State opinions on qualified alerts and national enforcement experiences is clearly stated, supporting information exchange and coordinated governance of general-purpose AI models.

chapters.summaryText: Establishes an advisory forum to provide technical expertise to the AI Board and Commission, with balanced stakeholder representation including industry, civil society, and academia.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The concept of 'balanced selection' and 'recognised expertise' involves subjective assessment by the Commission
Clearly establishes the advisory forum with defined purpose to provide expertise and advise the Board and Commission

chapters.heading: Article 68
chapters.title: Scientific panel of independent experts
chapters.fullTitle: Article 68: Scientific panel of independent experts
chapters.summaryText: Establishes a scientific panel of independent AI experts to support the AI Office in enforcement activities, particularly for general-purpose AI models with systemic risks, and defines expert qualifications, tasks, independence requirements, and operational procedures.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article mandates establishment of scientific panel via implementing act, but detailed procedures, selection criteria, number of experts, and operational arrangements are delegated to that implementing act and AI Office procedures.

referencesWithContext.context: g act shall be adopted in accordance with the examination procedure referred to in [Article 98 (2)](#article-98-2).
referencesWithContext.snippet: ination procedure referred to in Article 98 (2).

ambiguitySignal.reason: Obligation to establish scientific panel is clear, but all operational details (composition, procedures, selection) are delegated to implementing act adopted via Article 98(2) examination procedure.

referencesWithContext.context: ientific or technical expertise in the field of AI necessary for the tasks set out in [paragraph 3](#article-68-3), and shall be able to demonstrate meeting all of the following conditions:
referencesWithContext.snippet: necessary for the tasks set out in paragraph 3, and shall be able to demonstrate m

where.text: experts selected by the Commission must have up-to-date scientific or technical expertise in AI for tasks in paragraph 3

ambiguitySignal.reason: Expert qualification criteria require 'up-to-date scientific or technical expertise in AI' and ability to demonstrate meeting conditions in sub-points. 'Up-to-date' and demonstration standards left to Commission discretion; 'fair gender and geographical representation' is subjective.

Requirement for 'particular expertise and competence' and 'scientific or technical expertise in the field of AI' is not quantitatively defined. What constitutes 'particular' level is left to evaluator discretion.

Independence from 'any provider of AI systems or general-purpose AI models' requires interpretation. Does this mean no past employment, no financial interests, no consulting relationships? Scope and degree of independence not explicitly defined.

Ability to carry out activities 'diligently, accurately and objectively' is subjective and not measurably defined. 'Fair gender and geographical representation' lacks quantitative thresholds or proportions.

without_prejudice_to.text: the powers of market surveillance authorities

ambiguitySignal.reason: Panel tasks are enumerated explicitly: support AI Office on general-purpose AI models/systems enforcement, support market surveillance authorities upon request, support cross-border surveillance, and support Union safeguard procedures. Scope is well-defined.

referencesWithContext.context: - (a) supporting the implementation and enforcement of this Regulation as regards general-purpose AI models and systems, in particular by:
referencesWithContext.snippet: - (a) supporting the implementation and enforcement of this Regulatio

ambiguitySignal.reason: Panel tasked with supporting implementation/enforcement 'in particular by' alerting on systemic risks, contributing to evaluation tools/methodologies/benchmarks, and providing classification advice. 'In particular' suggests list is non-exhaustive; exact scope of 'contributing' and 'providing advice' is open to interpretation.

Panel shall support market surveillance authorities 'at their request'. What constitutes a valid request, response time obligations, scope of support provided, and prioritization among competing requests are not specified.

referencesWithContext.context: -c"> supporting cross-border market surveillance activities as referred to in [Article 74 (11)](#article-74-11), without prejudice to the powers of market surveillance authorities;

ambiguitySignal.reason: Panel supports AI Office in Union safeguard procedure duties per Article 81. Scope and procedures are clearly anchored to referenced article.

What constitutes impartial/objective conduct and adequate confidentiality measures requires interpretation. AI Office 'shall establish systems and procedures to actively manage and prevent conflicts' but specific mechanisms are not defined.

chapters.summaryText: Establishes Member States' access to the scientific panel experts for enforcement support, including fee structures and Commission coordination with Union AI testing support.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Permission is clearly stated with explicit actor (Member States) and scope (calling upon experts for enforcement support).

chapters.heading: Section 2
chapters.title: National competent authorities
chapters.fullTitle: Section 2: National competent authorities

chapters.heading: Article 70
chapters.title: Designation of national competent authorities and single points of contact
chapters.fullTitle: Article 70: Designation of national competent authorities and single points of contact
chapters.summaryText: Mandates Member States to designate national competent authorities (notifying and market surveillance) and single points of contact, ensuring they have adequate resources, independence, and cybersecurity measures, with periodic reporting obligations.

ambiguitySignal.reason: While designation and reporting obligations are explicit, terms like 'adequate resources', 'sufficient number of personnel', 'appropriate measures', and 'adequate level of cybersecurity' require contextual judgment. Annual assessment obligation in paragraph 3 provides some guardrail but interpretation varies.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Paragraph 2 and 6 specify explicit deadline of 2 August 2025 for contact information publication and first reporting

ambiguitySignal.reason: Core obligation to designate authorities is clear. However, requirements for 'independence', 'impartiality', 'without bias', and ensuring 'objectivity' are principles requiring contextual application. Flexibility clause ('may be performed by one or more designated authorities') introduces Member State discretion.

Multiple explicit obligations with clear temporal deadline (2 August 2025). Actions required are specific: communicate identity/tasks to Commission, publish contact info electronically, designate single point of contact, notify Commission, Commission publishes list.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Explicit deadline: Member States must make contact information publicly available by 2 August 2025

ambiguitySignal.reason: Requires 'adequate' resources and 'sufficient number of personnel' with enumerated competences, but 'adequate' and 'sufficient' are inherently discretionary and context-dependent. Annual assessment requirement provides some safeguard but no objective thresholds or metrics defined. Competence areas listed but depth/breadth not specified.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Annual assessment and update obligation begins upon application date

ambiguitySignal.reason: Requires 'appropriate measures' for 'adequate level of cybersecurity' without defining what is appropriate or adequate. No reference to specific standards (e.g., ISO 27001, NIS2 Directive). Highly discretionary and context-dependent.

referencesWithContext.context: national competent authorities shall act in accordance with the confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78
referencesWithContext.snippet: confidentiality obligations set out in Article 78

when.text: performing their tasks

ambiguitySignal.reason: Explicit cross-reference to Article 78 which defines the confidentiality obligations. Condition 'when performing their tasks' is broad but understood in regulatory context. Obligation is determinate.

Explicit temporal obligations: first report by 2 August 2025, then every two years. Reporting content specified (status of resources, adequacy assessment). Commission transmission to Board is mandatory. Clear procedural obligation.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: First report due by 2 August 2025, then biennial cycle

ambiguitySignal.reason: Obligation to 'facilitate exchange of experience' is intentionally broad. No specification of method, frequency, or format. Commission has wide discretion in how to fulfil this obligation (workshops, platforms, documents, etc.).

Permission to provide guidance creates discretion for authorities. Scope of 'implementation' guidance not strictly bounded. Requirement to 'take into account' Board/Commission guidance is soft. Consultation with other Union law authorities 'as appropriate' introduces discretionary element. However, overall permission structure is clear.

where.text: Union institutions, bodies, offices or agencies fall within the scope of this Regulation

ambiguitySignal.reason: Explicit designation: EDPS acts as competent authority for Union institutions/bodies/offices/agencies. Condition is whether they fall within regulation scope (determined by Articles 1-2). Clear allocative rule.

chapters.heading: Chapter IX
chapters.title: POST-MARKET MONITORING, INFORMATION SHARING AND MARKET SURVEILLANCE

chapters.heading: Section 1
chapters.title: Post-market monitoring
chapters.fullTitle: Section 1: Post-market monitoring

chapters.heading: Article 72
chapters.title: Post-market monitoring by providers and post-market monitoring plan for high-risk AI systems
chapters.fullTitle: Article 72: Post-market monitoring by providers and post-market monitoring plan for high-risk AI systems
chapters.summaryText: Requires providers of high-risk AI systems to establish, document, and maintain a proportionate post-market monitoring system based on a formal plan. The monitoring must continuously collect and analyse performance data to evaluate ongoing compliance, including interaction analysis where relevant.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 (general obligations of providers) → article-16 → this article as requirement for high-risk AI system management

ambiguitySignal.reason: The term 'proportionate' (paragraph 1) is subject to context-dependent interpretation. The obligation to include 'interaction with other AI systems' analysis in monitoring (paragraph 2) depends on what constitutes 'relevant' interaction. The requirement to achieve 'equivalent level of protection' (paragraph 4) for legacy systems leaves room for reasonable disagreement on equivalence metrics.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Implementing act on monitoring plan template to be adopted by 2025-02-02 (Article 72(3))

ambiguitySignal.reason: The term 'proportionate' lacks explicit criteria. Proportionality must be determined by reference to 'the nature of the AI technologies and the risks of the high-risk AI system', but these parameters themselves are context-dependent and subject to provider discretion.

chapters.heading: Article 73
chapters.title: Reporting of serious incidents
chapters.fullTitle: Article 73: Reporting of serious incidents

where.text: serious incident occurs

chapters.summaryText: Establishes mandatory incident reporting obligations for providers and deployers of high-risk AI systems. Defines reporting timelines, procedures, and investigative requirements; establishes market surveillance authority coordination and Commission guidance requirements; addresses scope limitations for systems subject to equivalent Union legislation.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → obligations on providers and deployers → this article

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article contains clear timing obligations but 'causal link' and 'reasonable likelihood' require judgment. Scope limitations for equivalent Union legislation create ambiguity about which incidents fall under this article vs. sectoral regimes. Investigation requirements reference 'necessary investigations' without further specification.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Core obligation is unambiguous: 'shall report any serious incident to market surveillance authorities.' Defined terms (serious incident, high-risk AI system, market surveillance authority) are cross-referenced in Article 3. Scope is geographically clear (Union market, Member States where incident occurred).

Second subparagraph refers to 'the period' without specification of how it should be adjusted.

chapters.heading: Article 75
chapters.title: Mutual assistance, market surveillance and control of general-purpose AI systems
chapters.fullTitle: Article 75: Mutual assistance, market surveillance and control of general-purpose AI systems

where.text: AI system is based on a general-purpose AI model

chapters.summaryText: Establishes mutual assistance framework between the AI Office and market surveillance authorities for oversight of general-purpose AI systems. Grants the AI Office supervisory powers over general-purpose AI systems developed by the same provider, requires cooperation on non-compliant systems, and creates an information access procedure to support compliance investigations.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article defines institutional relationships and procedural obligations, but several terms leave room for interpretation: 'market surveillance authority is unable to conclude investigation' (Article 75-3) depends on factual assessment; 'appropriate efforts' is qualified but not precisely defined; the application of EU 2019/1020 Chapter VI procedures 'mutatis mutandis' requires contextual judgment.

where.text: an AI system is based on a general-purpose AI model, and the model and the system are developed by the same provider

chapters.summaryText: The AI Office shall have powers to monitor and supervise compliance of general-purpose AI systems (where model and system are developed by the same provider) with obligations under this Regulation, exercising all powers of a market surveillance authority under EU 2019/1020.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The phrase 'Where an AI system is based on a general-purpose AI model' involves determination of what qualifies as 'based on' (scope of derivative systems); 'same provider' principle is clear but implementation in complex supply chains may need guidance. The delegation to EU 2019/1020 powers requires cross-referencing external regulation.

where.text: the relevant market surveillance authorities have sufficient reason to consider general-purpose AI systems that can be used directly by deployers for at least one purpose that is classified as high-risk pursuant to this Regulation to be non-compliant with the requirements laid down in this Regulation

chapters.summaryText: Market surveillance authorities shall cooperate with the AI Office to carry out compliance evaluations of general-purpose AI systems suspected to be non-compliant, and shall inform the Board and other market surveillance authorities of findings and actions.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The condition 'sufficient reason to consider' involves a judgment call about evidentiary standard; 'can be used directly by deployers for at least one purpose' requires determination of intended use and classification scope. The coordination mechanism ('inform the Board and other market surveillance authorities') lacks detailed procedural specification.

referencesWithContext.context: Market surveillance authorities shall safeguard the confidentiality of the information that they obtain in accordance with [Article 78](#article-78) of this Regulation.
referencesWithContext.snippet: shall safeguard the confidentiality of the information that they obtain in accordance with Article 78 of this Regulation.

where.text: a market surveillance authority is unable to conclude its investigation of the high-risk AI system because of its inability to access certain information related to the general-purpose AI model despite having made all appropriate efforts to obtain that information

chapters.summaryText: Market surveillance authorities unable to access information necessary for high-risk AI system compliance investigations may submit a reasoned request to the AI Office, which must supply relevant information within 30 days. Authorities must safeguard confidentiality per Article 78 and apply EU 2019/1020 Chapter VI procedures mutatis mutandis.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Multiple elements involve factual/discretionary judgment: 'unable to conclude investigation' depends on sufficiency assessment; 'all appropriate efforts' is qualified but lacks definition; 'any information that the AI Office considers to be relevant' grants significant discretion to the AI Office; 'mutatis mutandis' application of external procedures (EU 2019/1020 Chapter VI) requires interpretation of how those rules adapt to this context.

chapters.heading: Article 78
chapters.title: Confidentiality
chapters.fullTitle: Article 78: Confidentiality
chapters.summaryText: Establishes confidentiality obligations for Commission, market surveillance authorities, notified bodies, and other persons involved in applying the Regulation. Protects intellectual property, business information, security interests, and criminal proceedings. Authorizes confidential information exchange with third-country authorities under bilateral/multilateral agreements.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraphs 1-2 are clear obligations. Paragraph 3 contains interpretive language regarding 'sensitive operational data' and conditions for disclosure (jeopardise public/national security); delegated acts may clarify scope. Paragraph 5 references international agreements not fully specified in the regulation.

when.text: involved in the application of this Regulation

applicability.scope: respect confidentiality of information and data obtained in carrying out tasks and activities

ambiguitySignal.reason: Core obligation to respect confidentiality. Exceptions listed in sub-points are exhaustive. Modal verb 'shall' is unambiguous.
Specifies protected category (IP/trade secrets/source code) with explicit exception to Directive 2016/943. No ambiguity in scope.
Specifies legitimate purpose for protecting confidential information: effective implementation and supervisory activities. Clear scope and justification.
'Public and national security interests' is inherently broad and requires member state interpretation. Member states may differ on which information falls within this scope.
Specifies two distinct procedural contexts (criminal and administrative) where confidential information must be protected. Clear justification.
References Union or national law classification regimes but does not specify which categories qualify. Scope depends on member state classification procedures.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The concept of 'contrary to Union law' (article-81-1) and 'justified' (article-81-2) depends on fact-specific analysis. The relationship between national measures and Union law compliance requires case-by-case evaluation. Article 81-3 references harmonised standards but the determination of 'attributed to shortcomings' involves technical judgment.

chapters.heading: Article 83
chapters.title: Formal non-compliance
chapters.fullTitle: Article 83: Formal non-compliance

where.text: market surveillance authority finds formal non-compliance (CE marking, declaration, registration, representative, or technical documentation defects)

chapters.summaryText: Establishes a two-stage enforcement procedure for formal non-compliance with administrative requirements (CE marking, EU declaration of conformity, EU database registration, authorised representative appointment, and technical documentation availability). Market surveillance authorities must initially require providers to remedy the specific defect within a prescribed period; if non-compliance persists, authorities shall take proportionate measures including restriction, recall, or withdrawal from the market.

The prescribed period is within market surveillance authority discretion but not ambiguous in its intent.

chapters.heading: Article 84
chapters.title: Union AI testing support structures
chapters.fullTitle: Article 84: Union AI testing support structures
chapters.summaryText: Establishes that the Commission shall designate one or more Union AI testing support structures to perform designated tasks and provide independent technical or scientific advice in the area of AI on request.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The phrase 'one or more Union AI testing support structures' is flexible in terms of number and organizational structure. The reference to 'tasks listed under Article 21(6) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020' delegates specifics to external legislation. The scope of 'independent technical or scientific advice' is broad and may require interpretation in application.

The exact scope and number of structures to be designated ('one or more') is not specified. The reference to Article 21(6) of Regulation (EU) 2019/1020 externalizes the task definitions. 'Designate' suggests administrative action but specific selection criteria are not provided.

referencesWithContext.context: Without prejudice to the tasks referred to in paragraph 1, Union AI testing support structures shall also provide independent technical or scientific advice

chapters.summaryText: Establishes the right of any natural or legal person to lodge complaints with market surveillance authorities regarding alleged infringements of the Regulation. Specifies that such complaints must be taken into account for market surveillance activities and handled according to established procedures under Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article clearly establishes a procedural right for complainants and obligations for market surveillance authorities. Language is definitive ('shall be taken into account', 'shall be handled'). No discretionary or vague terms. Scope is well-defined by reference to Regulation (EU) 2019/1020.

temporalScope.notes: Article 85 falls under Chapter IX (Section 4: Remedies) which is not explicitly listed in Article 113's exceptions, therefore subject to general application date of 2 August 2026

chapters.heading: Article 86
chapters.title: Right to explanation of individual decision-making
chapters.fullTitle: Article 86: Right to explanation of individual decision-making
chapters.summaryText: Grants affected persons the right to obtain clear explanations from deployers regarding the role of high-risk AI systems in decisions that produce legal or similarly significant effects on their health, safety, or fundamental rights. Establishes exceptions for systems under Annex III point 2 and where Union or national law provides alternative safeguards.

chapters.summaryText: Article 87 incorporates the EU whistleblower protection framework (Directive 2019/1937) into the AI Act regulatory regime, applying it to reporting of AI Act infringements and protection of reporting persons.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The article entirely delegates substantive requirements to Directive (EU) 2019/1937, which is external to this Regulation. The scope of the delegation—what constitutes an 'infringement' for reporting purposes—is defined in the AI Act, but the procedural protections and mechanisms are determined by the referenced directive.

chapters.heading: Article 98
chapters.title: Committee procedure
chapters.fullTitle: Article 98: Committee procedure
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the committee procedure for implementing and delegating acts under this Regulation, referencing the examination procedure under Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 98 specifies a clear procedural framework by direct reference to Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. No discretionary language or undefined terms. The procedure is fully specified in the referenced regulation.
Direct obligation on the Commission to be assisted by a committee. The nature and function of the committee is fully specified by the reference to Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Conditional reference to the examination procedure defined in Article 5 of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011. The condition is explicit ('where reference is made to this paragraph') and the procedure is fully defined in the external regulation. This paragraph is widely referenced throughout the AI Act (11 incoming references).

chapters.title: PENALTIES
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter XII: PENALTIES

chapters.heading: Article 99
chapters.title: Penalties
chapters.fullTitle: Article 99: Penalties
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the three-tier penalty framework for AI Act violations. Maximum fines up to €35M/7% for Article 5 prohibitions, €15M/3% for high-risk obligations, €7.5M/1% for misleading information. Member States must establish penalty rules, enforce them effectively, and consider SME viability. Also sets procedural safeguards and reporting obligations.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article establishes clear three-tier penalty structure with specific monetary amounts and percentage thresholds. Procedural aspects and Member State obligations are explicitly stated.

where.text: in the case of SMEs, including start-ups

ambiguitySignal.reason: Explicit rule for SMEs and start-ups: lower of the percentage or fixed amount applies. Clear mathematical formula.

where.text: as appropriate

when.text: deciding whether to impose an administrative fine and when deciding on the amount of the administrative fine in each individual case

ambiguitySignal.reason: Lists ten discretionary factors for authorities to 'take into account' and 'give regard to, as appropriate' when deciding fines. Factors include qualitative assessments ('nature, gravity and duration', 'degree of cooperation', 'degree of responsibility') requiring significant judgment.
Requires assessing 'nature, gravity and duration' and 'consequences' — all qualitative, discretionary factors. What constitutes 'appropriate' consideration is left to enforcement authorities.
Concrete factor: whether other market surveillance authorities have already fined the same operator for the same infringement. Factual inquiry.
Whether infringements of other laws 'result from the same activity or omission' requires legal judgment, but the general concept (coordination across legal regimes) is clear.
Concrete, quantifiable factors: size, annual turnover, market share. Factual inquiry with objective metrics.
'Any other aggravating or mitigating factor applicable to the circumstances' is an open-ended discretionary catch-all. Financial benefits/losses require assessment but are more concrete.
'Degree of cooperation' is a qualitative, discretionary assessment. What constitutes sufficient cooperation or adequate remediation is not defined.
'Degree of responsibility' and adequacy of 'technical and organisational measures' are qualitative assessments requiring significant judgment.
Concrete factor: how authorities learned of the infringement, and whether the operator self-reported. Factual inquiry with clear implications (self-reporting may be mitigating).
Distinguishing 'intentional' vs 'negligent' conduct is a well-established legal concept but requires factual and legal assessment on a case-by-case basis.
Whether an operator has taken 'action to mitigate the harm' and the adequacy of such action are discretionary assessments.
Member States have full discretion to determine 'to what extent' administrative fines apply to their own public authorities. No harmonised standard is set.

when.text: depending on the legal system of the Member States

ambiguitySignal.reason: Member States may choose the institutional mechanism (courts or other bodies) for imposing fines, provided there is 'equivalent effect'. What constitutes 'equivalent effect' may require interpretation, but the general flexibility is clear.
Explicit requirement for 'appropriate procedural safeguards' including 'effective judicial remedies and due process'. These are well-established legal concepts with EU and national law frameworks.

when.text: on an annual basis

ambiguitySignal.reason: Explicit annual reporting obligation. Scope is clear: administrative fines issued, related litigation/proceedings. Temporal requirement is unambiguous.

chapters.heading: Article 100
chapters.title: Administrative fines on Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies
chapters.fullTitle: Article 100: Administrative fines on Union institutions, bodies, offices and agencies
chapters.summaryText: Establishes the European Data Protection Supervisor's authority to impose administrative fines on Union institutions and bodies for infringements of this Regulation. Fines range from EUR 750,000 to EUR 1,500,000 depending on violation severity, with procedural safeguards for due process and proportionality.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-100-2 (EUR 1,500,000 for Article 5 violations), article-100-3 (EUR 750,000 for other violations)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 100 applies specifically to Union institutions and agencies, creating a parallel but distinct penalty regime from Article 99. The discretion given to the EDPS in assessing 'all relevant circumstances' (paragraph 1) allows reasonable interpretation about severity of specific violations.

when.text: deciding whether to impose an administrative fine and when deciding on the amount of the administrative fine in each individual case

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 1 establishes mandatory consideration of 'all relevant circumstances' and a list of seven factors, but does not specify weights or algorithmic application. The EDPS retains discretion in how to apply these factors proportionately.

where.text: taking into account the purpose of the AI system concerned, as well as, where appropriate, the number of affected persons and the level of damage suffered by them

ambiguitySignal.reason: The point refers to 'nature, gravity and duration' of infringement and its consequences, requiring case-specific judgment about how to weigh these factors and define thresholds.

where.text: taking into account technical and organisational measures implemented by them

ambiguitySignal.reason: The point requires assessment of 'degree of responsibility' based on 'technical and organisational measures implemented', but does not specify what level of measures is expected or how implementation status affects the degree assignment.

where.text: any action taken by the Union institution, body, office or agency to mitigate the damage suffered by affected persons

ambiguitySignal.reason: The point is clear: mitigation actions reduce the fine amount. This is a straightforward mitigating factor.

where.text: in order to remedy the infringement and mitigate the possible adverse effects of the infringement, including compliance with any of the measures previously ordered by the European Data Protection Supervisor

ambiguitySignal.reason: The point requires assessment of 'degree of cooperation' with the EDPS. The standard is not explicitly defined (full cooperation vs. minimal cooperation).

where.text: any similar previous infringements by the Union institution, body, office or agency

ambiguitySignal.reason: The point refers to 'any similar previous infringements', but 'similar' is not defined. Determining similarity requires judgment about what constitutes a comparable violation.

where.text: in particular whether, and if so to what extent, the Union institution, body, office or agency notified the infringement

ambiguitySignal.reason: The point requires assessment of how an infringement 'became known' and whether the institution notified it. The benefit of self-reporting vs. external discovery is not quantified.

where.text: the annual budget of the Union institution, body, office or agency

ambiguitySignal.reason: The point is clear: the EDPS must consider the institution's annual budget when assessing proportionality of the fine.
