ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 4 clearly establishes procedural requirements: the EDPS must give the institution an opportunity to be heard, base decisions only on contested elements, and associate complainants closely with proceedings.

where.text: subject to the legitimate interest of individuals or undertakings in the protection of their personal data or business secrets

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 5 requires full respect of rights of defence and file access, but permits restrictions for legitimate interests in protecting personal data and business secrets. The balance between access and confidentiality requires case-by-case judgment.
Paragraph 6 contains two distinct rules: (a) fines go to the EU general budget, and (b) fines shall not affect the institution's effective operation. Both are clearly stated.

when.text: on an annual basis

ambiguitySignal.reason: Paragraph 7 clearly establishes an annual reporting obligation: the EDPS must notify the Commission of fines imposed and any related litigation.

chapters.heading: Article 101
chapters.title: Fines for providers of general-purpose AI models
chapters.fullTitle: Article 101: Fines for providers of general-purpose AI models
chapters.summaryText: Establishes administrative fine regime for providers of general-purpose AI models. Commission may impose fines up to €15M or 3% of annual global turnover (whichever is higher) for infringements, including failure to comply with information requests, inspection measures, or evaluation access. Fines must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article grants Commission discretion to impose fines ('may impose') for multiple distinct infringement categories (regulatory violations, non-compliance with requests, failure to provide access). Proportionality assessment criteria are specified but leave room for institutional judgment. Interaction with Article 113(2) exception requires reading in conjunction.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113-2 exception: Article 101 excluded from 2025-08-02 application
temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 101 delayed to 2026-08-02 per Article 113(2)

systemType: general-purpose AI model with systemic risk

ambiguitySignal.reason: Criterion 'intentionally or negligently' is standard in administrative law but application is fact-dependent. Phrase 'relevant provisions of this Regulation' is broad and refers back to entire regulation. Standard of 'proportionality and appropriateness' is evaluative.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113-2 exception delays Article 101 to general application date

ambiguitySignal.reason: References 'relevant provisions of this Regulation' broadly. Determination of what constitutes 'infringement' depends on interpretation of all substantive obligations across Chapters I-XII.

where.text: AI systems placed on the market or put into service before specified dates (2025, 2026, 2027)

chapters.summaryText: Article 111 establishes transitional compliance pathways for AI systems and general-purpose AI models already on the market before specified dates: large-scale IT system components by 2030, high-risk systems by 2030 (with public-authority exception), and general-purpose AI models by 2027. These timelines defer full regulatory compliance from the standard entry-into-force date.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Transitional article with multiple conditional timelines and exemptions. Key ambiguity: scope of 'significant changes in their designs' (Article 111-2) lacks definitive guidance; 'necessary steps' (Articles 111-2, 111-3) is discretionary; relationship to Article 5 exception in Article 113(3) requires careful cross-reading.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 111 itself is a transitional provision, establishing staggered compliance timelines: Annex X systems by 2030-12-31, high-risk systems by 2030-08-02 (or 2026-08-02 if significant design changes), general-purpose AI models by 2027-08-02.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Critical ambiguity: 'significant changes in their designs' is undefined and discretionary. Does this mean any change, material change, or change affecting the risk profile? The second sentence applies a stricter timeline (2030-08-02) only to 'high-risk AI systems intended to be used by public authorities' — criteria for identifying such systems is not fully articulated here.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: High-risk systems placed on market before 2026-08-02 are only subject to this Regulation if they undergo 'significant changes in their designs' as from 2026-08-02. Systems intended for public authority use must comply by 2030-08-02. Exemption from Article 5 enforcement deferred per Article 113(3).

This could permit staged compliance planning without full implementation by the deadline. Scope of 'obligations laid down in this Regulation' is broad and requires cross-reference to determine which specific obligations bind general-purpose AI models.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: General-purpose AI models placed on market before 2025-08-02 must comply with regulation obligations by 2027-08-02. This is a transitional deadline separate from the standard general application date (2026-08-02 per Article 113, general para.).

chapters.summaryText: Establishes Commission's periodic obligations to evaluate the AI Act's implementation: assessing Annex III and prohibited practices (Article 5) annually, reporting every four years on needed amendments to high-risk criteria and transparency requirements, and conducting comprehensive reviews every four years starting August 2, 2028. Requires the AI Office to develop methodology for risk-level evaluation and risk classifications.

ambiguitySignal.reason: While core reporting obligations are clear, scope of evaluation criteria (paragraphs 4, 11), timing preconditions ('by 2 August...and every four years'), and methodology standards for risk-level assessment (para 11) contain some discretionary language ('appropriate', 'objective and participative') that could be subject to interpretation.

chapters.heading: Article 113
chapters.title: Entry into force and application
chapters.fullTitle: Article 113: Entry into force and application
chapters.summaryText: Article 113 establishes the entry-into-force timeline and temporal scope framework for the entire AI Regulation. It defines staggered application dates for different chapters and articles: Chapters I-II apply from 2025-02-02, Chapter III Section 4, Chapter V, Chapter VII, Chapter XII, and Article 78 apply from 2025-08-02 (except Article 101), and Article 6(1) applies from 2027-08-02. All other provisions apply from 2026-08-02.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Article 113 establishes explicit, unambiguous temporal boundaries for all chapters and articles. Each application date is directly specified with no interpretive discretion required.

normMetadata.scope: Article 6(1) (Classification of AI systems as high-risk) and all corresponding obligations throughout the Regulation

ambiguitySignal.reason: While the temporal boundary (2 August 2027) is explicit, the phrase 'corresponding obligations in this Regulation' requires interpretive determination of which obligations throughout the Regulation correspond to Article 6(1). This requires cross-referencing other articles and analyzing their connection to the classification framework.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Three-year transition period: high-risk classification regime deferred until 2027-08-02 to allow industry preparation and compliance infrastructure development

annexes.heading: Annex I
annexes.title: Union harmonisation legislation

structuralMetadata.scopeDescription: Informational annex listing Union harmonisation legislation referenced by various articles

annexes.summaryText: Annex I lists Union harmonisation legislation from the New Legislative Framework and other frameworks that are referenced throughout the AI Act for conformity assessment purposes.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Annex I is informational and does not impose direct obligations

ambiguitySignal.reason: Annex I provides a straightforward, enumerated list of existing Union harmonisation legislation with specific directive/regulation citations and official journal references. No interpretive scope exists.

annexes.heading: Annex II
annexes.title: Criminal Offences
annexes.summaryText: Annex II enumerates 16 categories of criminal offences referenced in Article 5(1)(h)(iii) regarding the prohibition of AI systems intended to manipulate persons through subliminal techniques or deceptive practices related to criminal activities.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Annex II is a reference/definitional annex. Penalties attach to Article 5(1)(h) violations, not to this annex itself (see Article 99-3 for maximum tier penalties on Article 5 violations).

ambiguitySignal.reason: Annex II is a straightforward enumerated list of criminal offence categories. Each item is a distinct, well-defined legal category with established international law foundation (e.g., ICC crimes, UN conventions). No scope or applicability ambiguity arises from the list itself.

annexes.heading: Annex III
annexes.title: High-risk AI systems

referencesWithContext.context: In addition to the high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1, AI systems referred to in Annex III shall be considered to be high-risk.
referencesWithContext.snippet: AI systems referred to in [Annex III](#annex-iii) shall be considered to be high-risk.

annexes.summaryText: Annex III establishes the definitive list of eight high-risk AI system categories subject to Chapter III requirements. All AI systems falling within these categories are presumed high-risk unless they meet Article 6(3) derogation criteria. This annex is foundational to the Act's risk-based governance structure.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → article-6-2/6-3 (failure to correctly classify or incorrectly treat as non-high-risk)

ambiguitySignal.reason: Articles 6(3) and 6(4) allow derogation when systems do not pose 'significant risk of harm', creating classification discretion. 'Significant risk' is context-dependent and requires provider assessment before placement.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 111(2) allows existing high-risk systems placed before 2026-08-02 to continue operating until 2030-08-02, provided no significant design changes are made.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16/22/26 requirements for biometric high-risk systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Remote biometric identification' excludes verification-only systems, but boundary between identification and verification can be technical and context-dependent. 'Emotion recognition' scope undefined—systems inferring emotional states versus systems detecting facial expressions may face interpretive challenges.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16/17 quality/risk management for critical infrastructure AI

ambiguitySignal.reason: 'Critical digital infrastructure' and 'safety component' require reference to sectoral definitions. Boundary between safety-critical vs. non-critical infrastructure operations not clearly delineated. Different sectors may have disparate standards for what constitutes critical.

temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: Article 113(2) specifically accelerates Chapter III Section 4 (which includes registration of Annex III systems) to 2025-08-02, 1 year before general application.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16 requirements for educational AI systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: Definition of 'educational and vocational training institutions at all levels' could include private tutoring platforms, online courses, or proprietary training systems. Scope of 'learning outcomes' evaluation (formative vs. summative assessment) requires contextual interpretation. 'Prohibited behaviour' detection during tests may conflict with fair assessment principles.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16 transparency/monitoring for employment AI systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: Distinction between AI systems that 'analyse and filter' applications versus systems that merely 'rank' candidates could be technical/interpretive. Systems used for 'performance evaluation' may be subjective; threshold between monitoring and excessive surveillance not specified. Internal training systems vs. external recruiting platforms may have different compliance burdens.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16 requirements for essential services AI systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: Boundary between 'essential' and non-essential services not defined; different Member States may classify services differently (e.g., telehealth vs. traditional healthcare). Financial fraud detection carve-out creates interpretive line (fraud vs. risk assessment). Life/health insurance pricing may be subjective; 'risk assessment' scope unclear. Emergency triage systems require clinical validation standards not specified in the regulation.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16/74 requirements for law enforcement AI systems; article-74-8 designates special market surveillance authorities

ambiguitySignal.reason: Risk assessment terminology ('victim', 'offending') lacks precise threshold or methodology. Distinction between profiling as defined in Directive 2016/680 vs. other forms of risk assessment requires specialized legal interpretation. 'Reliability of evidence' is subjective and context-dependent across jurisdictions. Polygraph exclusion from verification-only use creates boundary interpretation.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16/74 requirements for migration/asylum AI systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: Risk assessments ('security risk', 'irregular migration risk', 'health risk') lack objective criteria or thresholds; highly context-dependent on Member State policy and third-country agreements. 'Irregular migration' definition depends on legal status determination standards that vary by jurisdiction. Health risk assessment for border entry requires epidemiological standards not specified in regulation.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 → failure to meet Article 16 requirements for justice/democratic process AI systems

ambiguitySignal.reason: Systems 'assisting judicial authorities' in legal research or interpretation are broadly defined; scope encompasses recommendation systems through full decision-making support. Electoral influence carve-out ('natural persons not directly exposed') is difficult to enforce—social media or campaign analytics may have indirect voter exposure. Definition of 'outcome influence' vs. information provision not specified.

annexes.heading: Annex IV
annexes.title: Technical documentation
annexes.summaryText: Technical documentation template for high-risk AI systems, specifying all mandatory elements that providers must include to demonstrate compliance with essential requirements set out in Chapter III Section 2.

penaltyExposure.traceability: article-99-4 (obligations on providers) → article-11 (technical documentation requirement) → annex-iv

ambiguitySignal.reason: Annex IV specifies mandatory minimum elements but allows flexibility in implementation details (e.g., 'design specifications', 'data requirements', 'testing procedures'). Actual compliance assessment deferred to notified bodies per Article 32.

Annex IV-1 requires 'general description' and 'name of the provider' but does not specify exact format, completeness threshold, or detail level. Sub-points (a)-(h) provide structure but allow flexibility in scope.

Requirements for 'intended purpose' and 'version... reflecting relation to previous versions' lack specificity regarding: scope of previous versions to document, format requirements for version history, or criteria for what constitutes a meaningful version change.

Requirement to describe 'how the AI system interacts with...hardware or software...not part of the AI system itself' lacks specificity on: scope of interactions to document (e.g., all possible integrations or only likely/tested ones), required depth of documentation, or threshold for what constitutes 'interaction'.
Clear requirement for versions of relevant software/firmware and any requirements related to version updates. Scope is unambiguous.

Requirement to describe 'all the forms in which the AI system is placed on the market or put into service' may be ambiguous regarding: whether all theoretical forms must be documented or only those actually used, what constitutes a distinct 'form', or level of detail required for each form description.

Requirement for 'description of the hardware on which the AI system is intended to run' lacks specificity on: minimum hardware specifications, supported configurations, optional vs. required components, or performance characteristics to document.

Requirement for 'photographs or illustrations showing external features, the marking and internal layout' applies only 'where the AI system is a component of products'. The term 'component of products' is not precisely defined, potentially creating ambiguity about applicability.

Requirement for 'basic description of the user-interface provided to the deployer' is vague regarding: what constitutes 'basic' (visual mockup vs. detailed functional specification), scope of UI features to describe, or how to document UI in cases where interface is context-dependent.

Requirement for 'instructions for use for the deployer, and a basic description of the user-interface...where applicable' is qualified by 'where applicable', creating ambiguity about applicability criteria and when this element may be omitted.

Conditional procedural requirement. Applies only when the AI system processes personal data. Clear scope and reference to applicable data protection instruments.
Procedural requirement for documentation references. Clear specification of information to include.
Conditional procedural requirement. Applies when a notified body is involved in the conformity assessment. Clear specification of information required when applicable.
Procedural requirement for declaration issuance metadata. Clear specification of issuing information and signatory details.

annexes.title: Conformity assessment procedure

annexes.summaryText: Annex VI specifies the internal control-based conformity assessment procedure for AI systems, requiring providers to verify quality management system compliance, assess technical documentation against essential requirements, and ensure consistency between design, development, and post-market monitoring.

penaltyExposure.traceability: Annex (procedural) - no direct penalty exposure per Article 99

ambiguitySignal.reason: The annex specifies procedural steps (verify compliance, examine documentation, assess consistency) but lacks specific technical thresholds or measurable criteria. Terms like 'established quality management system' and 'consistent with technical documentation' require interpretation of what satisfies compliance.

temporalScope.applicableFromBasis: article-113-2 (Chapter III Section 4 applies 2025-08-02, but annexes related to conformity assessment for general high-risk systems apply 2026-08-02)
temporalScope.transitionalProvisions: No specific transitional provisions for this annex in Article 113.

annexes.heading: Annex VII
annexes.title: Conformity of Technical Documentation and the Quality Management System
annexes.fullTitle: Annex VII: Conformity of Technical Documentation and the Quality Management System
annexes.summaryText: Establishes the conformity assessment procedure based on quality management system and technical documentation assessment. Providers must apply through a notified body, which examines quality systems and technical documentation, issues certificates upon conformity, and conducts ongoing surveillance of approved systems.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Detailed procedural framework with multiple conditional triggers (4.3-4.7 contain many 'where', 'where relevant', 'if applicable', 'upon request' clauses). Discretion in access to training data ('where appropriate', 'subject to security safeguards') and notified body decision-making (4.4 'further evidence or further tests') creates room for reasonable disagreement on exact application scope.
Definitional introduction. States what the procedure is in plain declarative language. No conditions, no operative obligations, no discretion.

referencesWithContext.context: 2. Overview
referencesWithContext.snippet: 2. Overview

ambiguitySignal.reason: Uses 'shall be examined' and 'subject to surveillance' with reference to Article 17 requirements. The phrase 'shall be examined' is clear, but the scope of examination and surveillance depends on Article 17's contents, creating an interpretive layer.

the modified quality management system continues to satisfy requirements or reassessment is necessary

ambiguitySignal.reason: Multiple compound obligations with detailed procedural steps (3.1-3.4). Point 3.2 uses 'shall determine whether it satisfies requirements' which is clear, but 'adequate and efficient' in 3.3 is a vague standard requiring judgment. Point 3.4 references 'whether reassessment is necessary' which delegates discretion to the notified body.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Heavy reliance on discretionary language: 'where relevant', 'appropriate', 'where necessary', 'reasonable means', 'upon reasoned request', 'limited to what is necessary'. Points 4.3, 4.4, 4.5 grant notified bodies substantial discretion in access to training data and testing methodology. Point 4.5's 'after all other reasonable means' is inherently vague. Point 4.6's requirement for 'specific considerations on data quality' when refusing is under-specified. These create multiple areas where compliance interpretation requires case-by-case judgment.

where.text: periodic audits to ensure provider maintains and applies quality management system

ambiguitySignal.reason: Uses clear imperative verbs ('shall allow', 'shall carry out', 'shall provide') and specifies actions (access to premises, periodic audits, information sharing). However, point 5.1 states 'purpose is to make sure' which is an outcome standard, and 'duly complies' in 5.1 is somewhat evaluative. Points 5.2 and 5.3 are more operationally specific but 'all necessary information' in 5.2 and 'periodic' audits lack precise definition.

annexes.title: Technical documentation for providers of general-purpose AI models

annexes.summaryText: Annex XI specifies the technical documentation requirements for providers of general-purpose AI models under Article 53(1)(a). It prescribes information to be provided by all providers (model description, development details, training data, computational resources, energy consumption) and additional information for providers of high-systemic-risk models (evaluation strategies, adversarial testing, system architecture).

penaltyExposure.traceability: Annex XI is referenced in Article 53 but is not itself a direct source of obligations subject to Article 99 penalties. Non-compliance with documentation requirements is enforced through Article 53 obligations, which may incur medium-tier penalties (€7.5M / 1% of turnover) under Article 99(5) if documentation is incomplete or misleading.

ambiguitySignal.reason: The annex prescribes information to be included 'as appropriate to the size and risk profile of the model' (line 2515), which grants some discretion to providers in determining what constitutes 'appropriate.' Additionally, terms like 'detailed description,' 'relevant information,' and 'where applicable' introduce flexibility in scope and granularity, though these align with standard regulatory practice. The technical specifications themselves (parameters, modalities, data characteristics) are clear.

ambiguitySignal.reason: Annex specifies 'at least' these elements but implementation depends on interpreter judgment regarding what constitutes sufficient 'description' and 'information' for each required element. Some terms (e.g., 'acceptable use policies', 'curation methodologies') are not strictly defined and allow discretionary interpretation.
